**PRIVILEGED AND CONFIDENTIAL --- ATTORNEY-CLIENT COMMUNICATION / ATTORNEY WORK PRODUCT**

**[MEMORANDUM]{.underline}**

**TO:** Gerald T. Fong, City Manager, City of Lakemont, Illinois

**FROM:** Rebecca A. Sinclair, Esq., Corporation Counsel, City of Lakemont

**DATE:** March 5, 2025

**RE:** Lakemont Central TIF District --- Summary of Parameters, Eligible Costs, Increment Projections, and Prevailing Wage Applicability in Connection with Proposed Lakemont Station Commons Development

**CC:** Paul R. Vasquez, Director, Community Development Department; Jonathan Ashford, Ashford, Keene & Mulvaney LLP (outside municipal counsel)

**[I. Purpose and Background]{.underline}**

This memorandum has been prepared at the request of City Manager Gerald T. Fong in connection with the City of Lakemont\'s ongoing negotiation of a development agreement with Granite City Development LLC (\"Developer\") for the proposed Lakemont Station Commons project. On February 18, 2025, the City Council adopted Resolution R-2025-017, conditionally approving the selection of the Developer through a competitive Request for Proposals process. The City Manager and Marcus J. Pellegrini, on behalf of the Developer, executed a negotiated term sheet on January 22, 2025, outlining the preliminary parameters of the proposed public-private partnership.

The purpose of this memorandum is fourfold: (1) to provide a comprehensive summary of the Lakemont Central TIF District\'s legal parameters and remaining duration; (2) to identify eligible reimbursable project costs under the Illinois Tax Increment Allocation Redevelopment Act, 65 ILCS 5/11-74.4-3 (the \"TIF Act\"); (3) to project the tax increment available to support the \$48 million TIF reimbursement cap negotiated in the term sheet; and (4) to analyze prevailing wage obligations triggered by the various forms of public financial assistance associated with the project.

The Lakemont Station Commons project is a mixed-use, transit-oriented development on approximately 12.7 acres (Parcels 12-24-300-015, -016, and -017) located at 1400--1550 Lakemont Station Boulevard, Lakemont, Illinois 60048, immediately adjacent to the Lakemont Central Metra station. The project carries a total estimated cost of approximately \$412 million, with total hard construction costs of approximately \$340 million to be delivered across four phases. The project will include residential towers, office space, retail, a hotel, structured parking, a public plaza, and a community center.

This memorandum is intended to inform the development agreement negotiations and should be reviewed in conjunction with the Phase II Environmental Site Assessment Summary prepared by Greenfield Environmental Consultants LLC (dated November 8, 2024) and the appraisal prepared by Crossland Appraisal Services (dated January 15, 2025, appraised value of \$18.4 million for the three subject parcels).

**[II. TIF District Establishment and Legal Framework]{.underline}**

**A. Establishing Ordinance and Statutory Authority**

The Lakemont Central TIF District was established by Ordinance No. 2019-42, adopted by the City Council on September 17, 2019, pursuant to the Illinois Tax Increment Allocation Redevelopment Act, 65 ILCS 5/11-74.4-1 *et seq.* The City of Lakemont is a home rule municipality with a population of approximately 87,000, exercising home rule powers under Article VII, Section 6 of the Illinois Constitution of 1970, in addition to the statutory TIF authority conferred by the TIF Act. The TIF district was designated on the basis of findings that the redevelopment project area qualified as a conservation area under the eligibility criteria then applicable under the TIF Act, including findings relating to obsolescence, deterioration, inadequate utilities, and environmental contamination. The Lakemont Central TIF District encompasses the project site and a number of surrounding parcels, including the area immediately adjacent to the Lakemont Central Metra commuter rail station.

**B. District Boundaries and Included Parcels**

The three subject parcels --- 12-24-300-015, 12-24-300-016, and 12-24-300-017 --- are located within the Lakemont Central TIF District boundaries. These parcels are currently owned by the City, having been acquired through tax forfeiture proceedings in 2016 (Parcels -015 and -016) and by direct purchase in 2018 (Parcel -017). The parcels are presently zoned M-2 (General Manufacturing) and will require rezoning to PD-TOD (Planned Development--Transit Oriented District) as required by Condition No. 5 of Resolution R-2025-017. The three parcels have a combined acreage of approximately 12.7 acres (553,212 square feet) and constitute the former Lakemont Ironworks manufacturing campus, which ceased operations in 2009. The parcels are to be consolidated into a single legal parcel prior to or concurrent with the land conveyance to the Developer under the forthcoming development agreement.

**C. TIF District Duration and Expiration**

**The Lakemont Central TIF District expires on December 31, 2042.** Under the TIF Act, the maximum statutory life of a TIF district is twenty-three (23) years from the date of the ordinance designating the redevelopment project area. Ordinance No. 2019-42 was adopted in 2019, and the 23-year statutory limit extends through 2042, consistent with the December 31, 2042 expiration date set forth in the establishing ordinance.

Upon expiration, all tax increment ceases to be allocated to the Special Tax Allocation Fund, and all overlapping taxing districts --- including Lakemont Community Unit School District 204, Lake County, and the Lakemont Park District --- resume receiving their full share of property tax revenue from parcels within the former TIF district.

This expiration date is a fixed statutory constraint that cannot be extended unilaterally by the City. An extension would require either amending state legislation to authorize a longer duration for this specific TIF district or the adoption of a new TIF district subject to entirely new eligibility findings --- a process that would take a minimum of twelve to eighteen months and would require a new demonstration that the area qualifies as a blighted, conservation, or industrial park conservation area at the time of redesignation.

As of the date of this memorandum, the remaining life of the TIF district from March 2025 is approximately seventeen (17) years and nine (9) months. As discussed at length in Section IV below, this finite window for increment collection imposes a meaningful constraint on the City\'s ability to deliver the full \$48 million TIF reimbursement cap, particularly when considered in combination with the Phase I property tax abatement and the phased construction schedule.

**[III. Eligible Redevelopment Project Costs]{.underline}**

**A. Statutory Definition of Eligible Costs**

Under 65 ILCS 5/11-74.4-3(q), \"redevelopment project costs\" eligible for TIF reimbursement include the following categories relevant to the Lakemont Station Commons project:

> 1\. Costs of studies, surveys, development plans, and professional services, including planning, engineering, architectural, legal, and environmental assessment services;
>
> 2\. Costs of land acquisition within the TIF district, including any write-down of land value conveyed below appraised fair market value;
>
> 3\. Costs of site preparation, including demolition, grading, soil stabilization, and environmental remediation;
>
> 4\. Costs of installation, repair, and construction of public infrastructure improvements, including streets, utilities, sewers, water mains, traffic signals, sidewalks, and curbs;
>
> 5\. Costs of construction of public improvements and facilities, including parking structures serving a public purpose, public plazas, and community facilities;
>
> 6\. Costs of job training and retraining programs;
>
> 7\. Financing costs, including interest on TIF obligations and capitalized interest during construction of TIF-eligible improvements; and
>
> 8\. Relocation costs (not applicable to this project).

Critically, private development costs --- including market-rate or affordable residential construction, private office and retail space, and privately operated hotel components --- are *not* eligible for TIF reimbursement. Only expenditures that fall within the enumerated statutory categories and serve a public purpose qualify for reimbursement from the Special Tax Allocation Fund.

**B. Application to Lakemont Station Commons --- Eligible Cost Categories**

The following project costs have been identified as eligible for TIF reimbursement under the statutory framework:

**Environmental Remediation (\$3,140,000).** Parcel 12-24-300-016 requires remediation of petroleum hydrocarbon contamination (BTEX compounds) at an estimated cost of \$2,800,000. Parcel 12-24-300-017 requires installation of an engineered barrier to address metals contamination at an estimated cost of \$340,000. Both are eligible under the site preparation and environmental remediation category.

**Off-Site Public Infrastructure (\$12,500,000).** The City has committed to funding the widening of Lakemont Station Boulevard, a new traffic signal at the Oak Street intersection, water main extension, and sanitary sewer upgrades. All such costs are clearly eligible as public infrastructure improvements.

**On-Site Infrastructure (\$8,300,000).** The Developer\'s on-site infrastructure expenditures --- including internal public streets, utility connections to the municipal system, and stormwater management facilities --- are eligible to the extent they serve a documented public purpose. The development agreement should require the Developer to substantiate the public character of each on-site infrastructure component.

**Public Parking Structure, Public Plaza, and Community Center (Phase IV).** The 600-space public parking structure, the 2.5-acre public plaza with landscaping and public art, and the 28,000-square-foot community center to be conveyed to the City upon completion are all eligible as construction of public improvements and facilities. The portion of the \$71 million Phase IV hard construction costs allocable to these public components will require detailed cost segregation.

**Structured Parking in Phases I and II.** The 480-space garage (Phase I) and 350-space garage (Phase II) are potentially eligible to the extent they are publicly accessible or contractually committed to public use. Further analysis of access agreements and deed restrictions may be required to confirm eligibility.

**Land Write-Down (\$9,200,000).** The difference between the \$18,400,000 appraised value (Crossland Appraisal Services, January 15, 2025) and the \$9,200,000 sale price constitutes an eligible acquisition cost write-down under the TIF Act.

**Professional Services and Planning Costs.** Costs incurred by the City in connection with project planning, legal services, engineering, and environmental review are eligible.

Total estimated eligible costs for this project are likely to exceed the \$48 million cap by a significant margin. Accordingly, the cap itself --- not the universe of eligible costs --- will be the binding constraint on TIF reimbursement. As discussed in Section IV, the actual constraint is the availability of increment revenue over the remaining TIF district life.

**C. Costs NOT Eligible for TIF Reimbursement**

The following categories of project costs are not eligible for TIF reimbursement and must be excluded from any reimbursement claim:

> • Private residential construction, whether market-rate or affordable units (though certain affordable housing incentives may be separately eligible under specific TIF Act provisions);
>
> • Private office and commercial construction;
>
> • Private retail tenant improvements;
>
> • Hotel construction and fit-out for private hotel operations;
>
> • Developer\'s profit, general overhead, or contingency allocable to private components.

This distinction must be clearly delineated in the development agreement\'s reimbursement provisions. Only documented eligible costs, supported by certified cost documentation, should be submitted for TIF reimbursement.

**[IV. Increment Projections and Reimbursement Capacity]{.underline}**

**A. Methodology and Assumptions**

Tax increment is the increase in property tax revenue generated by the assessed value of new improvements within the TIF district above the \"frozen\" base equalized assessed value (EAV) established at the time of TIF district adoption. Because the three project parcels reflect a pre-development condition --- a vacant former manufacturing campus now owned by the City --- the base EAV is relatively low, and new improvements will generate substantial incremental assessed value.

The following assumptions underlie the projections set forth below:

> • Composite property tax rate (all overlapping taxing districts): approximately \$7.50 per \$100 of EAV;
>
> • Assessment ratio: 33.33% of fair market value (standard Illinois assessment level for non-farm, non-residential property);
>
> • Phase I estimated fair market value upon completion: approximately \$155 million (based on \$105 million hard construction cost plus land value and soft costs);
>
> • Phase II, III, and IV values estimated proportionally based on construction costs and market comparables;
>
> • Annual reassessment growth: 2% escalation per annum.

These projections are estimates only. Actual increment will depend on county assessor valuations, tax rates set annually by overlapping taxing districts, market conditions, and actual construction timelines.

**B. Increment Projection Table --- Phase-by-Phase Ramp-Up**

The following table presents estimated annual TIF increment from Phase I Substantial Completion through the TIF District expiration, assuming no abatement reduction:

  ---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
  **Tax Year**      **Phase(s) Generating Increment**      **Est. Annual Increment**   **Cumulative Increment**
  ----------------- -------------------------------------- --------------------------- --------------------------
  2029              Phase I (first full assessment year)   \$1,800,000                 \$1,800,000

  2030              Phase I                                \$2,100,000                 \$3,900,000

  2031              Phase I + Phase II (partial)           \$2,800,000                 \$6,700,000

  2032              Phase I + Phase II (full year)         \$3,400,000                 \$10,100,000

  2033              Phase I + II + Phase III (partial)     \$3,700,000                 \$13,800,000

  2034              Phase I--III + Phase IV (partial)      \$4,000,000                 \$17,800,000

  2035              All Phases (first full year)           \$4,200,000                 \$22,000,000

  2036              All Phases                             \$4,300,000                 \$26,300,000

  2037              All Phases                             \$4,400,000                 \$30,700,000

  2038              All Phases                             \$4,500,000                 \$35,200,000

  2039              All Phases                             \$4,600,000                 \$39,800,000

  2040              All Phases                             \$4,700,000                 \$44,500,000

  2041              All Phases                             \$4,800,000                 \$49,300,000

  2042              All Phases (final TIF year)            \$4,900,000                 \$54,200,000
  ---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Under this base-case projection, cumulative increment reaches approximately \$49.3 million by end of 2041 and \$54.2 million by the TIF district\'s expiration in 2042. This would exceed the \$48 million cap, suggesting that the cap is reachable under favorable conditions.

**However, this base case does not account for the Phase I property tax abatement.** The term sheet provides for a 12-year declining abatement on Phase I: 75% abatement in Years 1--4 (2029--2032), 50% in Years 5--8 (2033--2036), and 25% in Years 9--12 (2037--2040). Because the abatement reduces the assessed value of Phase I improvements for property tax purposes, it directly reduces the increment the TIF district captures from Phase I during those years. Applying the abatement to the Phase I contribution to annual increment yields substantially lower early-year figures. Under an adjusted scenario incorporating the abatement, cumulative increment through 2042 is estimated at approximately **\$38 million to \$42 million** --- potentially falling \$6 million to \$10 million short of the \$48 million cap.

Moreover, the pay-as-you-go structure means the Developer bears the timing risk. The Developer fronts all eligible costs and is reimbursed only as increment is actually generated and collected by the City. If cumulative increment falls short of the \$48 million cap by district expiration, the Developer absorbs the shortfall unless the development agreement provides an alternative mechanism.

**C. Sensitivity Analysis and Risk Factors**

Several scenarios could affect the increment projections described above:

**Upside scenarios** include: (a) higher-than-expected assessed values driven by a strong commercial and residential real estate market; (b) accelerated phasing resulting in earlier increment generation; and (c) structuring the Phase I abatement in a manner that minimizes its impact on TIF-capturable increment, if legally permissible.

**Downside scenarios** include: (a) construction delays extending Phase III and Phase IV completion beyond 2034, reducing the number of years of full-buildout increment collection before district expiration; (b) assessment appeals or challenges that reduce EAV; (c) declining market values affecting property assessments; (d) changes in the composite property tax rate; and (e) the Phase I tax abatement reducing capturable increment as described above.

Under a worst-case timing scenario, if all phases experience maximum permitted timeline extensions, including 18-month force majeure delays, Phase IV could be completed as late as 2036 or 2037, leaving only five to six years of full-buildout increment collection. At approximately \$4.2 million per year from full buildout, this would generate only \$21 million to \$25 million from the full-buildout period, plus partial increment from earlier phases --- dramatically reducing the likelihood of reaching the \$48 million cap.

It is my recommendation that the development agreement include: (a) a priority structure for TIF reimbursement, as set forth in Section VI.B below, ensuring environmental remediation and public infrastructure costs are reimbursed first; (b) an express acknowledgment that TIF reimbursement is not a guaranteed obligation of the City but is limited to actual increment generated and deposited in the Special Tax Allocation Fund; (c) a covenant requiring the City to use good-faith efforts to seek a TIF district extension if cumulative reimbursement is tracking materially below projections at a specified benchmark date (e.g., 2038); and (d) an express provision stating that a shortfall in TIF reimbursement does not constitute a default by the City under the development agreement. I recommend that Paul R. Vasquez coordinate with outside counsel at Ashford, Keene & Mulvaney LLP on drafting these provisions.

**[V. Prevailing Wage Applicability]{.underline}**

**A. Statutory Framework --- Illinois Prevailing Wage Act**

The Illinois Prevailing Wage Act, 820 ILCS 130/0.01 *et seq.* (the \"PWA\"), requires the payment of prevailing wages to laborers, workers, and mechanics on all \"public works\" projects. Section 2 of the PWA defines \"public works\" as \"all fixed works constructed or demolished by any public body, or paid for wholly or in part out of public funds.\" The key operative phrase is **\"paid for wholly or in part out of public funds.\"** Illinois courts and the Illinois Department of Labor (\"IDOL\") have interpreted \"public funds\" broadly to encompass not only direct governmental expenditures but also TIF disbursements, land write-downs, tax abatements, fee waivers, and other forms of public financial assistance that reduce the cost of a private development project.

**B. Application to Lakemont Station Commons Components**

**Components clearly subject to the Prevailing Wage Act:**

> • All off-site public infrastructure improvements (\$12.5 million, City-funded) --- funded entirely by public funds and constructed for public use; unambiguously \"public works.\"
>
> • On-site infrastructure improvements (\$8.3 million, Developer-funded but partially TIF-reimbursable) --- to the extent reimbursed from TIF, these are \"paid for in part out of public funds.\"
>
> • Environmental remediation on Parcels -016 and -017 (\$3.14 million) --- if TIF-reimbursed, the remediation work is paid for in part out of public funds and triggers prevailing wage obligations.
>
> • Phase IV public parking structure, public plaza, and community center --- publicly funded improvements (TIF-reimbursable), with the community center to be conveyed to the City; clearly public works under any interpretation.

**Components where prevailing wage applicability is less clear:**

Phase I includes a residential tower and retail podium with \$105 million in hard construction costs. The Developer receives a land conveyance at a 50% discount (\$9.2 million write-down), a 12-year property tax abatement, and a permit fee waiver of \$920,000. The question is whether these incentives render Phase I construction \"paid for ... in part out of public funds.\"

IDOL has consistently taken the position that if a private project receives *any* form of public financial assistance --- including land write-downs, tax abatements, or fee waivers --- the entire project may be subject to prevailing wage requirements. While appellate case law is not entirely settled, the trend in recent decisions favors broad application of the PWA to projects receiving TIF assistance or other public subsidies. Phase II (which receives a permit fee waiver of \$480,000) presents a similar analysis. Phase III (residential and hotel) does not appear to receive direct public financial assistance beyond the overall TIF framework, though the fact that it will be constructed on land originally acquired by the Developer at a discounted price may extend prevailing wage exposure to that phase as well.

**Given the significant public financial assistance provided to this project** --- including a \$9.2 million land write-down, TIF reimbursement of up to \$48 million, a 12-year property tax abatement, and permit fee waivers totaling \$1.4 million --- **it is this office\'s recommendation that the Prevailing Wage Act likely applies to all construction activity on the project site**, not merely the TIF-reimbursed components.

An important distinction exists between (a) the statutory obligation imposed by the PWA and (b) a voluntary contractual commitment. The negotiated term sheet states that prevailing wages shall apply to \"all construction\" on the project site. If the development agreement incorporates this term, it converts a potentially debatable statutory question into an unambiguous contractual obligation. The City benefits from this clarity, ensuring prevailing wages for all workers on a City-supported project. However, the Developer should understand that this provision may increase construction costs for components --- such as market-rate residential units, private office space, and the hotel --- that might otherwise be argued to fall outside the statutory mandate.

I recommend that the development agreement specify the scope of prevailing wage applicability with precision and that outside counsel at Ashford, Keene & Mulvaney LLP be prepared to address the Developer\'s likely arguments during negotiation regarding the scope of coverage, particularly for purely private components. The City\'s strongest position is the broad interpretation that any project receiving TIF assistance, land write-downs, or other public financial assistance triggers prevailing wage for the entire project.

**C. Prevailing Wage Monitoring and Enforcement**

The development agreement should include the following prevailing wage compliance provisions:

> • A Developer covenant requiring all general contractors and subcontractors to comply with the Prevailing Wage Act for all project construction;
>
> • Mandatory submission of certified payroll records to the City on a monthly basis;
>
> • A City right to audit contractor and subcontractor payroll records upon reasonable notice;
>
> • A Developer indemnification of the City for any claims, penalties, or liabilities arising from prevailing wage violations on the project;
>
> • Specified remedies for non-compliance, including withholding of TIF reimbursement payments.

**[VI. TIF Reimbursement Mechanics --- Pay-As-You-Go Structure]{.underline}**

**A. Reimbursement Process**

Under the negotiated terms, TIF reimbursement will be on a pay-as-you-go basis. The Developer will front all eligible costs and submit periodic reimbursement requests to the City, accompanied by certified cost documentation. The City will reimburse the Developer from the Special Tax Allocation Fund as increment is collected. Because property taxes are levied in one year and collected the following year, there is typically a twelve- to eighteen-month lag between property assessment and actual receipt of increment funds. The \$48 million cap is the maximum aggregate TIF reimbursement over the life of the TIF district. Reimbursement is not a general obligation of the City --- it is payable solely from available increment in the Special Tax Allocation Fund, and the City has no obligation to make payments from general revenue or any other source.

**B. Recommended Priority of Reimbursement**

Given the timing risk identified in Section IV, I recommend that the development agreement establish the following priority order for TIF reimbursement:

> 1\. **First Priority:** Environmental remediation costs for Parcels -016 and -017 (\$3,140,000). This prioritization aligns with the City\'s interest in ensuring contamination is addressed promptly and mitigates the City\'s indemnification exposure for pre-existing environmental conditions.
>
> 2\. **Second Priority:** Public infrastructure costs, including both the City\'s \$12.5 million commitment and the Developer\'s eligible on-site infrastructure expenditures.
>
> 3\. **Third Priority:** Public parking structure, public plaza, and community center costs associated with Phase IV.
>
> 4\. **Fourth Priority:** All other eligible costs, including professional services and financing costs.

This structure ensures that the most critical public-benefit costs are reimbursed first, even if total cumulative increment falls short of the \$48 million cap.

**[VII. Interaction with Other Public Incentives]{.underline}**

The TIF reimbursement must be understood in the context of the total public incentive package:

**Land Write-Down (\$9,200,000).** The write-down is itself an eligible TIF cost. If the City records it as a TIF expenditure from the Special Tax Allocation Fund, it counts against the \$48 million cap. I recommend that the development agreement clearly specify whether the land write-down is charged against the TIF cap or constitutes a separate incentive funded from another source.

**Property Tax Abatement (Phase I, 12-Year Declining).** As discussed in Section IV.B, the abatement reduces the assessed value of Phase I improvements, which in turn reduces the increment generated by Phase I. This creates an internal tension: the abatement benefits the Developer by reducing its property tax burden, but it simultaneously reduces the TIF increment available for Developer reimbursement. The development agreement must acknowledge this interaction explicitly.

**Permit Fee Waivers (\$1,400,000).** These are a separate incentive and should not be charged against the \$48 million TIF cap.

**City Infrastructure Commitment (\$12,500,000).** If funded from general revenue or capital improvement funds rather than TIF, this commitment does not reduce the TIF cap available for Developer reimbursement. If funded from TIF, it counts against the cap. The funding source should be specified in the development agreement.

The total incentive package --- \$9.2 million (land), up to \$48 million (TIF), \$12.5 million (infrastructure), \$1.4 million (fee waivers), plus the abatement value --- represents a substantial public investment. The development agreement should include robust clawback provisions in the event the Developer fails to deliver the project in accordance with the agreed-upon scope, timeline, and public benefit commitments.

**[VIII. Recommendations for Development Agreement Negotiations]{.underline}**

Based on the foregoing analysis, I offer the following recommendations for the City Manager and the Director of Community Development to carry into the development agreement negotiations:

**1. TIF Reimbursement Cap and Contingency.** The \$48 million cap may not be fully reachable given the December 31, 2042 district expiration and the impact of the Phase I tax abatement on increment generation. The development agreement should: (a) make TIF reimbursement expressly contingent on actual increment collected, not a guaranteed City obligation; (b) establish the reimbursement priority structure outlined in Section VI.B; (c) include a covenant requiring the City to use good-faith efforts to explore a TIF district extension if reimbursement is tracking materially below projections as of a specified benchmark date; and (d) expressly state that a TIF shortfall does not constitute a default by the City.

**2. Prevailing Wage Scope.** The development agreement should clearly delineate which construction components are subject to the Prevailing Wage Act by operation of statute and which are subject by contractual agreement pursuant to the term sheet\'s \"all construction\" language. The City should insist on prevailing wage coverage for all construction to eliminate ambiguity, but should be prepared for the Developer\'s counsel to resist the scope of coverage for purely private components. The City\'s strongest position is the broad interpretation that any project receiving TIF assistance, land write-downs, or other public funds triggers prevailing wage for the entire development.

**3. Environmental Remediation Priority.** Confirm that environmental costs (\$3.14 million) are documented as eligible TIF costs and prioritized for early reimbursement given the City\'s indemnification exposure for pre-existing contamination conditions.

**4. Abatement-TIF Interaction.** Consider whether the Phase I tax abatement can be structured to minimize its impact on TIF increment generation --- for example, by applying the abatement only to the non-TIF-captured portion of the tax bill, if legally permissible --- or, alternatively, include an express acknowledgment in the development agreement that the abatement reduces TIF reimbursement capacity and that the Developer accepts this trade-off.

**5. Outside Counsel Coordination.** Coordinate with Jonathan Ashford at Ashford, Keene & Mulvaney LLP on drafting the TIF-related provisions of the development agreement, with particular attention to the pay-as-you-go reimbursement mechanics, eligible cost documentation requirements, and shortfall risk allocation.

**6. Certified Cost Documentation.** Require the Developer\'s auditing firm, Clearmont Accounting Group LLP, to provide certified cost documentation supporting all TIF reimbursement claims, subject to City audit rights.

**[IX. Conclusion]{.underline}**

The Lakemont Central TIF District provides a significant but finite funding mechanism for the Lakemont Station Commons project. The \$48 million reimbursement cap is supported by eligible costs well in excess of that amount, but actual reimbursement will depend on increment generation, which is constrained by the December 31, 2042 district expiration and reduced by the Phase I property tax abatement. Under a base-case scenario without abatement impact, the cap appears reachable; under an adjusted scenario incorporating the abatement, cumulative increment may fall \$6 million to \$10 million short. Prevailing wage requirements apply broadly to this project given the substantial level of public financial assistance. The development agreement must address these issues with precision and allocate risk appropriately between the City and the Developer.

I am available to discuss this memorandum in further detail and to participate in drafting sessions for the development agreement at your convenience. Please do not distribute this memorandum outside the City Manager\'s office without prior consultation with this office.

Rebecca A. Sinclair, Esq. Corporation Counsel City of Lakemont, Illinois

"""Generate a .docx from markdown via Pandoc with an optional reference template.

Usage:
    python generate_from_md.py input.md output.docx [template.docx]

Pandoc applies the template's paragraph and run styles to your markdown.
Best for reports/memos/letters where the template carries firm styling.

Requires pandoc on PATH.
"""
import subprocess
import sys
from pathlib import Path


def generate(md_path: Path, output_path: Path, template_path: Path | None = None):
    output_path.parent.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True)
    cmd = ["pandoc", str(md_path), "-o", str(output_path)]
    if template_path is not None:
        cmd.append(f"--reference-doc={template_path}")
    result = subprocess.run(cmd, capture_output=True, text=True)
    if result.returncode != 0:
        print(f"pandoc failed: {result.stderr}", file=sys.stderr)
        sys.exit(1)
    print(f"OK: wrote {output_path}")


if __name__ == "__main__":
    if len(sys.argv) not in (3, 4):
        print("Usage: generate_from_md.py <input.md> <output.docx> [template.docx]", file=sys.stderr)
        sys.exit(2)
    template = Path(sys.argv[3]) if len(sys.argv) == 4 else None
    generate(Path(sys.argv[1]), Path(sys.argv[2]), template)

"""Validate a .docx for delivery.

Checks ZIP integrity, XML well-formedness for every part, [Content_Types].xml
presence, and relationship consistency (no dangling rIds). Does NOT do XSD
schema validation in v1 — defer until ECMA-376 XSDs are vendored fresh from
ECMA-International.

Usage: python validate.py file.docx
Exit 0 = valid; non-zero = errors printed to stderr.
"""
import sys
import zipfile
from pathlib import Path
from lxml import etree


def validate(path: Path) -> list[str]:
    errors = []

    if not path.exists():
        return [f"File not found: {path}"]
    if not zipfile.is_zipfile(path):
        return [f"Not a valid ZIP: {path}"]

    with zipfile.ZipFile(path) as z:
        names = set(z.namelist())

        if "[Content_Types].xml" not in names:
            errors.append("Missing [Content_Types].xml")
            return errors

        # XML well-formedness for every .xml and .rels part
        for name in names:
            if name.endswith(".xml") or name.ends

{"file_path": "/workspace/output/drafting-inventory.md", "content": "# Drafting Inventory — Lakemont Station Commons Development Agreement
(Developer's counsel working notes; not a deliverable)

## Source documents
1. city-council-resolution-r2025-017.docx — City Council conditional approval + 8 conditions (a–h)
2. negotiated-term-sheet.docx — Non-binding term sheet (Developer/City, 1/22/25) — principal business terms
3. construction-loan-term-sheet.docx — First Prairie National Bank preliminary (non-binding) loan term sheet ($210M)
4. lakeshore-side-letter.docx — Lakeshore Capital (25% member) side letter: consent rights, anti-dilution, 14% IRR, 51% CoC
5. phase-ii-esa-summary.docx — Greenfield Phase II ESA (11/8/24): Parcel 015 clean; 016 BTEX+TCE ($2.8M, 14–18 mo); 017 metals fill ($340K)
6. rfp-response-narrative.docx — Developer RFP response (12/16/24)
7. tif-district-summary-memo.docx — Corporation Counsel privileged TIF memo (3/5/25)

## Cross-document CONFLICTS to flag (bracketed drafting notes)

C1. **Reversionary interest / clawback (CENTRAL).** Resolution Cond. #8(h) requires Developer to grant City a reversionary interest (auto-revert if Phase I not SC within 36 mo of conveyance). Loan TS §§4.2, 12 PROHIBIT any reversionary/defeasible fee — would make loan impossible to close; requires alternative (repurchase option, escrow/LC, liquidated damages, enhanced guaranty, ROFR). Lakeshore SL §7(d) requires Investor consent for any clawback and compatibility with Lender. Term Sheet is SILENT on reversion. → Draft alternative clawback; flag as open item requiring City agreement to deviate from Resolution.

C2. **36-month reversion clock vs. 30-month construction.** Resolution measures 36 months from CONVEYANCE; Phase I SC deadline is 9/30/2028 with groundbreaking by 3/1/2026. If conveyance is mid-2025, 36 months may expire BEFORE the SC deadline. → Flag timing mismatch; reinforces eliminating reversion.

C3. **Assignment / change-of-control thresholds.** Term Sheet §10: permitted assignment only to Pellegrini-controlled (≥50%) entity; City consent otherwise. Loan TS §8.1: CoC if Pellegrini <[50]% (bracketed), or ceases as Manager/CEO, or new investor ≥25%. Lakeshore SL §5.2: CoC if Pellegrini Interest <51%, or Pellegrini ceases as Manager/CEO (not replaced in 60 days w/ Investor-approved person), or new person ≥25%, or merger. → Three thresholds (50% / [50]% / 51%). Harmonize via Key Person + carve-outs + 60-day cure (Lakeshore §8.3).

C4. **Parcel acquisition history.** Resolution/TIF memo/RFP: 015+016 tax forfeiture 2016; 017 direct purchase 2018. ESA §1.3: 015 tax forfeiture 2016; 016+017 direct purchase 2018. → Conflict; matters for title/encumbrance review.

C5. **Parcel acreages.** RFP: 015 ~3.8 ac, 016 ~4.1 ac, 017 ~4.8 ac. ESA: 015 ~4.2 ac, 016 ~4.1 ac, 017 ~4.4 ac. Both total ~12.7 ac. → Minor conflict.

C6. **RFP number.** RFP narrative: No. 2024-CD-07. Term Sheet: No. 2024-CD-018. → Conflict.

C7. **County.** ESA §1.3: "Cook County PINs." RFP §3.1 + TIF memo §V.B: Lake County (Recorder; IDOL prevailing wage for Lake County). → Conflict; affects recording/consolidation jurisdiction.

C8. **Affordable unit count.** Resolution: 106 (20% of 530). Term Sheet §12 summary table: "at least 100"; detailed breakdown 64+42=106. → Reconcile to 106 (exceeds 20% min and "at least 100" floor).

C9. **CGL insurance aggregate.** Term Sheet §11.3: $10M occ / $20M agg. Loan TS §5: $10M occ / $25M agg. → Reconcile to higher ($25M).

C10. **TIF shortfall risk.** Term Sheet §5.2: pay-as-you-go, no guarantee of full $48M. TIF memo: adjusted for abatement, cumulative increment ~$38–42M, possibly $6–10M short; recommends shortfall NOT a City default; good-faith extension covenant; priority structure. → Developer interest: priority, extension covenant, shortfall not Developer's risk/default.

C11. **Land write-down vs. TIF cap.** TIF memo §VII: if $9.2M write-down charged against TIF cap, reduces Developer reimbursement. → Developer: write-down NOT charged against cap.

C12. **City infrastructure funding source vs. TIF cap.** TIF memo: if $12.5M City infra funded from TIF, counts against cap. → Developer: fund from general/capital, not TIF.

C13. **Prevailing wage scope.** Term Sheet §11.1: "all construction." TIF memo: broad application to ALL construction (City position); Developer may resist purely private components. → Open item; cost risk.

C14. **Environmental cost allocation / overruns.** ESA §8.2: City primary responsible party (pre-existing, City owned w/ knowledge); recommends clear cost allocation + 25–35% overrun provision (Parcel 016 exposure $3.5–3.8M; total $3.8–4.2M). Term Sheet §6: City indemnifies pre-existing; remediation TIF-eligible; silent on overruns/performer. Lakeshore SL §4.3(d): Investor consent if env obligations >$3.14M without offset. → Developer: City bears overruns / TIF-reimbursable; phased conveyance.

C15. **NFR vs. RAP as condition precedent; phased conveyance.** Resolution Cond. #8(b): IEPA-approved RAP before ANY conveyance. Loan TS §7.1(7): Parcel 016 — NFR OR (VSRP enrollment + approved RAP + $3.5M escrow/insurance). ESA §5.2: 14–18 mo timeline; recommends phased conveyance (015/017 first). Term Sheet §4: RAP approval condition to closing. → Reconcile CP (RAP approval, not full NFR); permit phased conveyance.

C16. **Equity demonstration deadline.** Resolution Cond. #8(a): within 60 days of resolution adoption (by 4/19/2025). Term Sheet §7: within 60 days of City Council approval of definitive Agreement. → Conflict; Developer prefers later trigger.

C17. **Performance security for later phases.** Resolution/TS: $15M for Phase I; later-phase amounts "to be established." → Open item.

C18. **Community benefits — living wage + first-source.** Resolution Cond. #8(c): CBA w/ (i) 35% local hiring, (ii) living wage $17.50/hr permanent retail/hotel, (iii) first-source w/ Lakemont Workforce Center. Term Sheet §9: local hiring goal only (good-faith, not default). RFP §6.2: first-source + 35%. → Living wage NEW obligation not in Term Sheet; flag.

C19. **Local hiring — goal vs. requirement.** Term Sheet: 35% goal, good-faith, NOT default. Resolution: "requiring that not less than 35%." → Reconcile to goal/good-faith (Developer-favorable).

C20. **LEED "or equivalent" acceptability.** Resolution: Director Vasquez determines. Term Sheet: City in writing. → Developer: objective standard.

C21. **Force majeure cap.** Term Sheet §14: 18-month aggregate. Loan TS §9(9): 180-day (6 mo) force majeure for Phase I SC default. → Gap; align; flag.

C22. **Dispute resolution.** Term Sheet §17: negotiation→mediation (Lakemont Mediation Center)→binding AAA arbitration (Cook/Lake). Loan TS §13: Cook County courts (litigation). Lakeshore SL §10.2: negotiation→AAA mediation→AAA arbitration. → DA uses arbitration; note loan uses litigation.

C23. **Condemnation 15%.** Term Sheet §15: Developer may terminate if >15% by non-City entity. Loan TS §9(8): >15% = loan default. → Align; note City-condemnation gap.

C24. **Substantial Completion definition.** Term Sheet §13: to be defined; generally CO issuance. → Define precisely (Developer-favorable).

C25. **Lender protections (Loan TS §12).** (a) no reversion; (b) Lender notice + cure (30/60 days beyond Borrower); (c) Lender consent to material amendments; (d) subordination of City interests; (e) foreclosure/deed-in-lieu assignment w/o City consent (or not unreasonably withheld, 30-day); (f) estoppel certificates (2x/yr). → Must incorporate; flag City negotiation items.

C26. **Lakeshore consent rights over DA.** SL §4: review/approve final DA form (15 bd before execution); consent for Material Amendments; specific consents (TIF cap, abatement, phasing >2035, env >$3.14M, perf security, assignment/CoC). → Developer internal constraint; harmonize DA terms.

C27. **Minimum IRR 14%.** Lakeshore SL §3. → Developer cannot agree DA terms materially impairing IRR w/o Investor consent; flag.

C28. **Property tax abatement — Phase I only.** Term Sheet §5.4/18.4: Phase I only; later phases "to be addressed." → Developer: preserve; flag later-phase abatement open item.

C29. **Community center conveyance.** Term Sheet §3.4: conveyed "at no additional cost," designed w/ City Parks & Rec. RFP §5.5: "fully furnished and equipped per agreed specifications." → Define scope; cap obligation; open item (specs).

C30. **Vapor intrusion / mitigation (Parcel 016).** ESA §§7.1, 8.1: vapor screening + possible mitigation (sub-slab, vapor barriers). Not in Term Sheet/Resolution. → Developer: City-borne or TIF-reimbursable (pre-existing).

C31. **ELUC / institutional controls as encumbrances.** ESA: ELUCs on 016 (groundwater) + 017 (barrier). Loan TS §4.2: permitted exceptions if Lender-counsel approved. → Ensure Permitted Exceptions; City indemnity.

C32. **Off-site groundwater monitoring on Metra property.** ESA §8.3: recommends off-site wells on Metra property; needs Metra access. → Open item; City assist; cost TIF/City.

C33. **Rezoning before DA execution.** Resolution Cond. #8(e) + Term Sheet §18.2: rezoning complete/effective before DA execution. Loan TS §7.1(2): non-appealable before loan closing. → Consistent; Developer: DA contingent on rezoning.

C34. **Parcel consolidation.** Resolution: consolidate before/concurrent w/ rezoning. ESA: distinct chains of title; review before consolidation; ELUCs complicate. → Open item; City delivers clean consolidated title.

C35. **City indemnity scope/survival (environmental).** Term Sheet §16: City indemnifies pre-existing; survives termination. ESA: pre-existing, City knowledge. → Broaden; survive; coordinate w/ insurance.

C36. **TIF reimbursement mechanics.** Term Sheet: pay-as-you-go, Developer fronts. TIF memo: 12–18 mo lag; priority recommended; certified cost docs; City audit. → Developer: priority, interest on delay, reasonable audit.

C37. **TIF extension good-faith covenant.** TIF memo rec: City good-faith effort to seek extension if tracking low by benchmark (e.g., 2038). Not in Term Sheet. → Developer: include covenant.

C38. **Reporting overlap.** Term Sheet: quarterly construction (30 days) + quarterly local hiring. Lakeshore: monthly construction (15 days), quarterly fin (45), annual audited (90), correspondence copies, default notice (5 bd), TIF copies. Loan: monthly draws, annual audited (120), quarterly (45). → Harmonize.

C39. **Cross-default DA↔Loan.** Loan TS §9(5): DA material default/termination = loan default. → Coordinate cure; flag.

C40. **Loss of entitlements.** Loan TS §9(10): loss of PD-TOD/TIF/env approvals = default. → Developer: if City-caused, force majeure/extension, not Developer default.

## OPEN ITEMS (not conflicts; unresolved terms)
O1. Hotel brand/flag (Term Sheet §3.3: "to be determined").
O2. Architect & GC selection (RFP §2.4; Loan TS §7.1(12)(13): Lender-approved).
O3. Later-phase financing $58.4M (Term Sheet §7; Loan TS §3; Lakeshore §5) — not secured; Lender+Investor consent.
O4. Performance security amounts Phases II–IV.
O5. Community center design specs / furnishings.
O6. Public parking operating arrangements / access agreements (TIF eligibility).
O7. Metra access for off-site monitoring.
O8. Substantial Completion precise definition.
O9. "Permitted Exceptions" to title (to be agreed).
O10. Form of ELUCs (Lender/title approval).
O11. GMP/stipulated-sum construction contract terms.
O12. Sources & Uses final; soft-cost allocation.
O13. Take-out/permanent financing commitment (Loan TS §2.4: 6 mo before maturity).
O14. Guaranty terms (completion + partial repayment burn-down) (Loan TS §1).
O15. Interest reserve funding (Loan TS §2.2)."}

Lakeshore SL §7(d) requires Investor consent for any clawback and compatibility with Lender. Term Sheet is SILENT on reversion. → Draft alternative clawback; flag as open item requiring City agreement to deviate from Resolution.

C2. **36-month reversion clock vs. 30-month construction.** Resolution measures 36 months from CONVEYANCE; Phase I SC deadline is 9/30/2028 with groundbreaking by 3/1/2026. If conveyance is mid-2025, 36 months may expire BEFORE the SC deadline. → Flag timing mismatch; reinforces eliminating reversion.

C3. **Assignment / change-of-control thresholds.** Term Sheet §10: permitted assignment only to Pellegrini-controlled (≥50%) entity; City consent otherwise. Lakeshore SL §5.2: CoC if Pellegrini Interest <51%, or Pellegrini ceases as Manager/CEO (not replaced in 60 days w/ Investor-approved person), or new person ≥25%, or merger. → Three thresholds (50% / [50]% / 51%). Harmonize via Key Person + carve-outs + 60-day cure (Lakeshore §8.3).

C4. **Parcel acquisition history.** Resolution/TIF memo/RFP: 015+016 tax forfeiture 2016; 017 direct purchase 2018. ESA §1.3: 015 tax forfeiture 2016; 016+017 direct purchase 2018. → Conflict; matters for title/encumbrance review.

C5. **Parcel acreages.** RFP: 015 ~3.8 ac, 016 ~4.1 ac, 017 ~4.8 ac. ESA: 015 ~4.2 ac, 016 ~4.1 ac, 017 ~4.4 ac. Both total ~12.7 ac. → Minor conflict.

C8. **Affordable unit count.** Resolution: 106 (20% of 530). Term Sheet §12 summary table: "at least 100"; detailed breakdown 64+42=106. → Reconcile to 106 (exceeds 20% min and "at least 100" floor).

C9. **CGL insurance aggregate.** Term Sheet §11.3: $10M occ / $20M agg. Loan TS §5: $10M occ / $25M agg. → Reconcile to higher ($25M).

C10. **TIF shortfall risk.** Term Sheet §5.2: pay-as-you-go, no guarantee of full $48M. TIF memo: adjusted for abatement, cumulative increment ~$38–42M, possibly $6–10M short; recommends shortfall NOT a City default; good-faith extension covenant; priority structure. → Developer interest: priority, extension covenant, shortfall not Developer's risk/default.

C11. **Land write-down vs. TIF cap.** TIF memo §VII: if $9.2M write-down charged against TIF cap, reduces Developer reimbursement. → Developer: write-down NOT charged against cap.

C12. **City infrastructure funding source vs. TIF cap.** TIF memo: if $12.5M City infra funded from TIF, counts against cap. → Developer: fund from general/capital, not TIF.

C13. **Prevailing wage scope.** Term Sheet §11.1: "all construction." TIF memo: broad application to ALL construction (City position); Developer may resist purely private components. → Open item; cost risk.

C14. **Environmental cost allocation / overruns.** ESA §8.2: City primary responsible party (pre-existing, City owned w/ knowledge); recommends clear cost allocation + 25–35% overrun provision (Parcel 016 exposure $3.5–3.8M; total $3.8–4.2M). C15. **NFR vs. RAP as condition precedent; phased conveyance.** Resolution Cond. #8(b): IEPA-approved RAP before ANY conveyance. Loan TS §7.1(7): Parcel 016 — NFR OR (VSRP enrollment + approved RAP + $3.5M escrow/insurance). ESA §5.2: 14–18 mo timeline; recommends phased conveyance (015/017 first). Term Sheet §4: RAP approval condition to closing. → Reconcile CP (RAP approval, not full NFR); permit phased conveyance.

C16. **Equity demonstration deadline.** Resolution Cond. #8(a): within 60 days of resolution adoption (by 4/19/2025). Term Sheet §7: within 60 days of City Council approval of definitive Agreement. → Conflict; Developer prefers later trigger.

C17. **Performance security for later phases.** Resolution/TS: $15M for Phase I; later-phase amounts "to be established." → Open item.

C18. **Community benefits — living wage + first-source.** Resolution Cond. #8(c): CBA w/ (i) 35% local hiring, (ii) living wage $17.50/hr permanent retail/hotel, (iii) first-source w/ Lakemont Workforce Center. C24. **Substantial Completion definition.** Term Sheet §13: to be defined; generally CO issuance. → Define precisely (Developer-favorable).

C25. **Lender protections (Loan TS §12).** (a) no reversion; (b) Lender notice + cure (30/60 days beyond Borrower); (c) Lender consent to material amendments; (d) subordination of City interests; (e) foreclosure/deed-in-lieu assignment w/o City consent (or not unreasonably withheld, 30-day); (f) estoppel certificates (2x/yr). → Must incorporate; flag City negotiation items.

{"content": "# DEVELOPMENT AGREEMENT

## LAKEMONT STATION COMMONS MIXED-USE TRANSIT-ORIENTED DEVELOPMENT

**by and between**

**GRANITE CITY DEVELOPMENT LLC**
(an Illinois limited liability company, "Developer")

**and**

**CITY OF LAKEMONT, ILLINOIS**
(an Illinois home rule municipality, "City")

[Drafting Note — Purpose and posture of this draft. This Development Agreement ("Agreement") is drafted by Developer's counsel, Thornfield & Pratt LLP, on behalf of Granite City Development LLC, and reflects the Developer's positions and interests. It is based principally on the non-binding Negotiated Term Sheet dated January 22, 2025 (the "Term Sheet"), as conditioned by City Council Resolution No. R-2025-017 adopted February 18, 2025 (the "Resolution"). Bracketed drafting notes in this format [Drafting Note — ...] identify (i) cross-document conflicts among the Term Sheet, the Resolution, the First Prairie National Bank preliminary construction loan term sheet (the "Loan Term Sheet"), the Lakeshore Capital Partners Fund III LP side letter dated February 10, 2025 (the "Lakeshore Side Letter"), the Phase II Environmental Site Assessment executive summary prepared by Greenfield Environmental Consultants LLC dated November 8, 2024 (the "Phase II ESA"), the Developer's RFP response narrative dated December 16, 2024 (the "RFP Response"), and the Corporation Counsel TIF district summary memorandum dated March 5, 2025 (the "TIF Memo"), and (ii) open items requiring further negotiation, third-party input, or factual confirmation. Drafting notes are not operative contract text and are intended to be deleted or converted to a separate issues list prior to execution.]

---

## ARTICLE 1. RECITALS AND DEFINITIONS

### Section 1.1 Recitals.

**1.1.1** The City is the owner of certain real property commonly known as 1400–1550 Lakemont Station Boulevard, Lakemont, Illinois 60048, consisting of three (3) parcels of record identified by Parcel Identification Numbers 12-24-300-015, 12-24-300-016, and 12-24-300-017, totaling approximately 12.7 acres (553,212 square feet) (collectively, the "Property" or "Site"). The Property constitutes the former site of the Lakemont Ironworks manufacturing campus, which ceased industrial operations in 2009, and is situated immediately adjacent to the Lakemont Central Metra commuter rail station on the Metra Milwaukee District/North Line.

[Drafting Note — Conflict C4 (parcel acquisition history). The Resolution, the TIF Memo, and the RFP Response state that the City acquired Parcels 12-24-300-015 and 12-24-300-016 through tax forfeiture proceedings concluded in 2016, and acquired Parcel 12-24-300-017 by direct purchase in 2018. The Phase II ESA (Section 1.3) states that Parcel 12-24-300-015 was acquired by tax forfeiture in 2016, while Parcels 12-24-300-016 and 12-24-300-017 were acquired by direct purchase in 2018. This discrepancy is material because tax-forfeiture parcels may carry different title characteristics (e.g., unresolved tax claims, different warranty posture) than direct-purchase parcels, which affects the City's ability to deliver insurable title and the scope of the City's special warranty deed covenants. Confirm the acquisition history for each parcel and reconcile the recital accordingly. The Phase II ESA (Section 1.3) further recommends a comprehensive per-parcel title review before consolidation to identify any environmental liens, utility easements, unresolved tax claims, or mechanic's liens arising from the City's prior demolition/clearing work.]

[Drafting Note — Conflict C5 (parcel acreages). The RFP Response (Section 3.1) states Parcel 015 is approximately 3.8 acres, Parcel 016 approximately 4.1 acres, and Parcel 017 approximately 4.8 acres. The Phase II ESA (Section 1.3) states Parcel 015 is approximately 4.2 acres, Parcel 016 approximately 4.1 acres, and Parcel 017 approximately 4.4 acres. Both total approximately 12.7 acres. The per-parcel acreage should be confirmed by the ALTA/NSPS survey required under the Loan Term Sheet (Section 7.1(5)) and stated precisely in the legal description exhibit.]

[Drafting Note — Conflict C7 (county jurisdiction). The Phase II ESA (Section 1.3) refers to "Cook County Parcel Identification Numbers," while the RFP Response (Section 3.1) refers to coordination with the "Lake County Recorder's Office" and the TIF Memo (Section V.B) references prevailing wage rates for "Lake County." The county in which the Property is located must be confirmed, as it determines the recording jurisdiction for the deed, consolidation plat, affordability covenants, environmental land use controls ("ELUCs"), and the mortgage, and the applicable Illinois Department of Labor prevailing wage schedule. Reconcile and state the correct county throughout.]

**1.1.2** The Property is currently zoned M-2 (General Manufacturing) under the City of Lakemont Zoning Ordinance. The proposed mixed-use transit-oriented development requires rezoning of the Property to PD-TOD (Planned Development–Transit Oriented District).

**1.1.3** The City conducted a competitive Request for Proposals process for the redevelopment of the Property.

[Drafting Note — Conflict C6 (RFP number). The RFP Response identifies the solicitation as "RFP No. 2024-CD-07," while the Term Sheet (preamble) identifies it as "RFP No. 2024-CD-018." Confirm the correct RFP number and state it consistently.]

Developer was selected as the preferred developer based upon its qualifications, experience, project proposal, and demonstrated financial capacity. Developer's principal, Marcus J. Pellegrini, has completed projects including the Halsted Collection (Chicago), Riverside Crossing (Naperville), and Archer Park Residences (Chicago). Developer's reported entity-level net worth is approximately $185,000,000 (audited by Clearmont Accounting Group LLP), with bonding capacity of up to $75,000,000 through Ridgepoint Surety Corporation.

**1.1.4** The ownership structure of Developer is as follows: Pellegrini Family Trust — 60%; Lakeshore Capital Partners Fund III LP, a Delaware limited partnership whose general partner is Lakeshore Capital Management LLC (Managing Partner: Richard "Rick" Dominguez) — 25%; and TransitWorks Equity Group LLC — 15%.

**1.1.5** Developer proposes a four-phase mixed-use transit-oriented development to be known as "Lakemont Station Commons" (the "Project"), with a total gross building area of approximately 1,620,000 square feet, total estimated hard construction cost of approximately $340,000,000, and total estimated Project cost (inclusive of soft costs) of approximately $412,000,000.

**1.1.6** The Property was appraised at a fair market value of $18,400,000 on an as-is basis by Crossland Appraisal Services in an appraisal report dated January 15, 2025. The City proposes to convey the Property to Developer at a purchase price of $9,200,000, representing 50% of the appraised fair market value, resulting in a land write-down incentive of $9,200,000.

**1.1.7** The Property is located within the Lakemont Central Tax Increment Financing District, established pursuant to Ordinance No. 2019-42, which expires on December 31, 2042. The City proposes to pledge up to $48,000,000 in tax increment financing revenues to reimburse Developer for eligible redevelopment project costs on a pay-as-you-go basis.

**1.1.8** A Phase II Environmental Site Assessment was completed by Greenfield Environmental Consultants LLC in a report dated November 8, 2024, identifying environmental conditions on the Property as further described in Article 6.

**1.1.9** The City Manager and Developer executed the non-binding Term Sheet dated January 22, 2025. The City Council adopted the Resolution on February 18, 2025, granting conditional approval and authorizing the City Manager to negotiate and execute this Agreement subject to the conditions set forth in Section 3 of the Resolution.

**1.1.10** Developer has obtained a preliminary (non-binding) commitment from First Prairie National Bank (the "Construction Lender") for a construction loan facility of up to $210,000,000, subject to execution of this Agreement in form and substance acceptable to the Construction Lender. Developer's committed equity sources include the Pellegrini Family Trust ($34,000,000) and Lakeshore Capital Partners Fund III LP ($51,000,000), for total committed equity of $85,000,000.

### Section 1.2 Definitions.

In addition to terms defined elsewhere in this Agreement, the following terms have the meanings set forth below. Capitalized terms used but not defined herein have the meanings ascribed to them in the Term Sheet.

"**Affordable Unit**" means a residential dwelling unit restricted to occupancy by households earning at or below sixty percent (60%) of Area Median Income.

"**AMI**" means Area Median Income as published annually by the U.S. Department of Housing and Urban Development for the Chicago-Naperville-Elgin, IL-IN-WI Metropolitan Statistical Area, or any successor designation.

"**BTEX**" means benzene, toluene, ethylbenzene, and xylene compounds.

"**Change of Control**" has the meaning set forth in Section 15.2.

"**Construction Lender**" means First Prairie National Bank, or any successor construction lender approved in accordance with the Loan Documents and this Agreement.

"**Construction Loan**" means the construction loan facility of up to $210,000,000 provided by the Construction Lender.

"**ELUC**" means an environmental land use control recorded against the Property in connection with the IEPA Voluntary Site Remediation Program.

"**IEPA**" means the Illinois Environmental Protection Agency.

"**Loan Term Sheet**" means the preliminary construction loan term sheet provided by First Prairie National Bank, as the same may be amended, supplemented, or superseded by the Construction Loan Documents.

"**Pellegrini Interest**" means the aggregate direct and indirect membership interest in Developer held by Marcus J. Pellegrini and/or the Pellegrini Family Trust, whether held of record or beneficially.

"**Phase**" or "**Project Phase**" means each of Phase I, Phase II, Phase III, and Phase IV of the Project, as described in Article 3.

"**Project Budget**" means the detailed line-item budget for the Project set forth in Exhibit [__], including hard costs, soft costs, contingencies, environmental remediation, infrastructure, and interest reserve.

"**RAP**" means a Remedial Action Plan submitted to and approved by IEPA under the Voluntary Site Remediation Program.

"**Substantial Completion**" has the meaning set forth in Section 12.2.

"**TIF**" or "**Tax Increment Financing**" means tax increment financing administered under the Illinois Tax Increment Allocation Redevelopment Act, 65 ILCS 5/11-74.4-1 et seq. (the "TIF Act").

"**TIF District**" means the Lakemont Central TIF District established by Ordinance No. 2019-42, expiring December 31, 2042.

"**VSRP**" means the IEPA Voluntary Site Remediation Program.

## ARTICLE 2. EFFECTIVENESS; CONDITIONS PRECEDENT TO EXECUTION

### Section 2.1 Effectiveness.

This Agreement shall become effective upon the date of its full execution by both parties and approval by the Lakemont City Council at a duly noticed public meeting (the "Effective Date").

### Section 2.2 Conditions Precedent to Execution.

The execution and delivery of this Agreement by Developer is subject to the satisfaction (or waiver by Developer) of the following conditions precedent:

(a) **Rezoning.** The rezoning of the Property from M-2 (General Manufacturing) to PD-TOD (Planned Development–Transit Oriented District) shall have been approved by the City Council by ordinance and shall be final and effective, with the applicable appeal period expired without the filing of any appeal, or any appeal finally resolved in a manner that confirms the rezoning.

(b) **Parcel Consolidation.** The consolidation of Parcels 12-24-300-015, 12-24-300-016, and 12-24-300-017 into a single parcel of record shall have been completed or authorized by the applicable county authority, subject to the title review recommended in the Phase II ESA.

(c) **IEPA Remedial Action Plan.** IEPA shall have approved a RAP for Parcel 12-24-300-016 under the VSRP, addressing the BTEX and TCE contamination identified in the Phase II ESA.

(d) **Equity Demonstration.** Developer shall have demonstrated committed equity of not less than $85,000,000 in form and substance reasonably acceptable to the City.

(e) **City Council Approval.** The Lakemont City Council shall have approved this Agreement.

(f) **Construction Lender Confirmation.** The Construction Lender shall have confirmed in writing that this Agreement is in form and substance acceptable to it as a condition of the Construction Loan commitment.

The Resolution's earlier deadline controls as a condition of the City's authorization. Developer should confirm whether the April 19, 2025 demonstration has been or will be timely made, and whether the City will accept the Term Sheet's later trigger. The condition in Section 2.2(d) is stated generally; the timing should be reconciled. Developer's preference is the later Term Sheet trigger, but the Resolution is the controlling City action.]

[Drafting Note — Conflict C15 (NFR vs. RAP as condition precedent; phased conveyance). ## ARTICLE 3. THE PROJECT

### Section 3.1 Project Description.

The Project consists of a mixed-use, transit-oriented development organized in four phases, with a total gross building area of approximately 1,620,000 square feet, comprising the program elements set forth in this Article 3 and in Exhibit [__] (Project Description and Phasing Plan). The Project is designed as a cohesive, master-planned transit-oriented development serving as a catalyst for revitalization of the Lakemont Central station area.

### Section 3.2 Phase I — Residential Tower A and Retail Podium.

Phase I comprises: (a) three hundred twenty (320) residential dwelling units, consisting of two hundred fifty-six (256) market-rate units and sixty-four (64) Affordable Units; (b) forty-five thousand (45,000) square feet of ground-floor retail space; and (c) a four hundred eighty (480)-space structured parking facility. Phase I comprises approximately four hundred twenty thousand (420,000) square feet of gross building area, with an estimated hard construction cost of $105,000,000. Phase I groundbreaking shall occur no later than March 1, 2026, with Substantial Completion by September 30, 2028 (approximately thirty (30) months of construction).

### Section 3.3 Phase II — Office Building and Retail.

Phase II comprises: (a) two hundred eighty thousand (280,000) square feet of Class A office space; (b) eighteen thousand (18,000) square feet of retail space; and (c) a three hundred fifty (350)-space structured parking facility. Phase II comprises approximately three hundred eighty thousand (380,000) square feet of gross building area, with an estimated hard construction cost of $76,000,000. Phase II shall commence within twelve (12) months of Phase I Substantial Completion, with Substantial Completion within twenty-four (24) months of Phase II commencement.

### Section 3.4 Phase III — Residential Tower B and Hotel.

Phase III comprises: (a) two hundred ten (210) residential dwelling units, consisting of one hundred sixty-eight (168) market-rate units and forty-two (42) Affordable Units; and (b) a one hundred fifty (150)-room select-service hotel. Phase III comprises approximately four hundred forty thousand (440,000) square feet of gross building area, with an estimated hard construction cost of $88,000,000. Phase III shall commence within twelve (12) months of Phase II Substantial Completion, with Substantial Completion within thirty (30) months of Phase III commencement.

[Drafting Note — Open item O1 (hotel brand). The Term Sheet (Section 3.3) states the hotel brand affiliation is "to be determined" and that Developer is in discussions with two national hospitality flags. The Agreement should preserve Developer's flexibility to select the hotel brand based on prevailing market conditions at the time of Phase III execution, subject only to compliance with the PD-TOD zoning and the LEED Gold commitment. No City approval of the specific brand should be required.]

### Section 3.5 Phase IV — Public Plaza, Community Center, and Parking Structure.

Phase IV comprises: (a) a 2.5-acre public plaza incorporating landscaping and public art installations; (b) a twenty-eight thousand (28,000) square foot community center facility to be conveyed to the City upon completion as provided in Section 3.7; and (c) a six hundred (600)-space public parking structure. Phase IV comprises approximately three hundred eighty thousand (380,000) square feet of gross building area, with an estimated hard construction cost of $71,000,000. Phase IV shall commence concurrently with Phase III, with Substantial Completion within twenty-four (24) months of Phase IV commencement.

### Section 3.6 Project Summary Totals.

In the aggregate, the Project shall include: five hundred thirty (530) residential dwelling units, of which one hundred six (106) shall be Affordable Units; sixty-three thousand (63,000) square feet of total retail space; two hundred eighty thousand (280,000) square feet of Class A office space; one hundred fifty (150) hotel rooms; one thousand four hundred thirty (1,430) structured parking spaces; a 2.5-acre public plaza; and a 28,000 square foot community center. The total estimated hard construction cost for all four phases is approximately $340,000,000, and the total estimated Project cost, inclusive of soft costs, is approximately $412,000,000.

[Drafting Note — Conflict C8 (affordable unit count). The Resolution and the detailed breakdown in Term Sheet Section 12 specify 106 Affordable Units (64 in Phase I + 42 in Phase III), representing 20% of 530 total residential units. The Term Sheet Section 3.5 summary table, however, states "at least 100 affordable units." The Agreement states 106 units, which exceeds both the City's 20% minimum (Resolution Condition #8(g)) and the "at least 100" floor in the Term Sheet summary. Confirm the City accepts 106 as the binding number and that the "at least 100" language in the Term Sheet summary is superseded.]

### Section 3.7 Community Center Conveyance.

The community center shall be designed in consultation with the City's Parks and Recreation Department. Upon Substantial Completion of Phase IV, Developer shall convey the community center to the City in fee simple by special warranty deed, at no additional cost to the City beyond the costs included in the Project Budget. The community center shall be delivered in accordance with the design specifications agreed by the parties in Exhibit [__].

[Drafting Note — Conflict C29 (community center scope). The Term Sheet (Section 3.4) states the community center is "to be designed in consultation with the City's Parks and Recreation Department and conveyed to the City upon completion at no additional cost." The RFP Response (Section 5.5) states the community center will be conveyed "fully furnished and equipped per the agreed-upon specifications." The scope of "furnished and equipped" and the meaning of "at no additional cost" (i.e., no cost beyond the Project Budget line item) must be precisely defined in the specifications exhibit to avoid open-ended Developer obligations. Open item O5 (community center design specifications/furnishings) must be resolved and attached as an exhibit. Developer's position: the conveyance obligation is limited to the building shell and core and the furnishings/equipment expressly itemized in the agreed specifications, all within the Project Budget; "at no additional cost" means no cost beyond amounts already budgeted, not that Developer will absorb unbudgeted scope.]

### Section 3.8 Public Parking Structure.

The 600-space public parking structure in Phase IV shall serve Metra commuters, retail and restaurant patrons, community center and plaza visitors, and Project residents and workers, and shall be publicly accessible. The operating and access arrangements for the public parking structure shall be set forth in an access/operating agreement in Exhibit [__], which shall confirm the public character of the facility for TIF eligibility purposes.

[Drafting Note — Open item O6 (public parking operating arrangements). The TIF Memo (Section III.B) notes that the Phase IV public parking structure, public plaza, and community center are eligible as construction of public improvements and facilities, and that the Phase I (480-space) and Phase II (350-space) garages are "potentially eligible to the extent they are publicly accessible or contractually committed to public use," requiring analysis of access agreements and deed restrictions. The operating/access agreements should be structured to maximize TIF eligibility while preserving Developer's reasonable operational control. Developer should confirm the desired public-access commitments for each parking facility.]

### Section 3.9 Design Review.

Developer shall submit design plans for each Phase to the City's Community Development Department for review and approval. The City's review shall be limited to confirming consistency with the PD-TOD zoning standards, the Project Description, and applicable building codes. The City shall approve or provide written comments within thirty (30) business days of submission; approval shall be deemed given if the City does not respond within such period. The City's approval rights shall not extend to aesthetic matters within Developer's discretion or to matters that would increase Project cost or delay the schedule, in each case without the City bearing the resulting cost or extending equivalent schedule relief.

[Drafting Note — Developer-protective provision. The Term Sheet (Section 19) contemplates "design review and approval procedures" in the definitive Agreement. From the Developer's perspective, design review should be (i) bounded to zoning/code consistency, (ii) subject to a deemed-approval default, and (iii) accompanied by cost/schedule protection so that City-requested changes are at City cost or with schedule relief. The City may seek broader design review authority; this is an open negotiation item.]
