## ARTICLE 4. LAND CONVEYANCE

### Section 4.1 Conveyance; Purchase Price.

Following the Effective Date and satisfaction of the conditions precedent to closing set forth in Section 4.3, the City shall convey the Property (as consolidated into a single parcel, or in phased conveyances as provided in Section 4.2) to Developer by special warranty deed, free and clear of all liens, encumbrances, and exceptions except Permitted Exceptions. The purchase price is $9,200,000, representing 50% of the appraised as-is market value of $18,400,000 (Crossland Appraisal Services, dated January 15, 2025), resulting in a land write-down incentive of $9,200,000. The purchase price shall be due and payable at closing, and Developer may fund it from equity, Construction Loan proceeds, or a combination thereof.

### Section 4.2 Phased Conveyance.

Notwithstanding any other provision of this Agreement, Developer may elect, by notice to the City, to receive conveyance of the Property in phases: (a) Parcel 12-24-300-015 (which requires no remediation) and Parcel 12-24-300-017 (subject to completion of the engineered barrier) may be conveyed first; and (b) Parcel 12-24-300-016 may be conveyed after issuance of the NFR Letter for that parcel. A phased conveyance shall be coordinated with the PD-TOD rezoning, parcel consolidation, and the Construction Lender's requirements regarding the scope and legal description of the mortgaged premises.

[Drafting Note — Conflict C15/C36 (phased conveyance). The Phase II ESA (Sections 4.1.3, 5.2, 8.2) recommends a phased conveyance approach to allow Phase I construction to begin on clean Parcels 015 and 017 while Parcel 016 remediation proceeds, noting that this requires (i) PD-TOD rezoning and consolidation to accommodate phased delivery or separate/interim land use approvals, (ii) resolution of per-parcel title issues, and (iii) coordination with the Construction Lender's mortgage scope. Resolution Condition #8(b) requires IEPA approval of the RAP for Parcel 016 "prior to any conveyance of the Property or any portion thereof," which on its face permits conveyance of clean parcels once the RAP is approved (it does not require the NFR Letter before conveyance of any parcel). The City should confirm that phased conveyance is permitted and that the rezoning/consolidation can accommodate it. This is a key schedule-risk mitigation for Developer.]

### Section 4.3 Conditions Precedent to Closing.

The obligation of each party to close the conveyance is subject to satisfaction (or waiver by the waiving party) of the following conditions precedent:

(a) Execution and delivery of this Agreement and City Council approval;
(b) Completion of rezoning of the Property from M-2 to PD-TOD;
(c) Completion or authorization of parcel consolidation (or, in the case of a phased conveyance, the applicable parcel(s));
(d) IEPA approval of the RAP for Parcel 12-24-300-016;
(e) Developer's demonstration of committed equity of at least $85,000,000;
(f) Developer's posting of the $15,000,000 Phase I performance security under Article 8;
(g) Delivery by the City of an owner's title insurance commitment insuring marketable and insurable fee simple title in Developer, subject only to Permitted Exceptions, together with a current ALTA/NSPS land title survey; and
(h) Delivery by the City of the Construction Lender's confirmation that this Agreement is in form acceptable to it.

### Section 4.4 Title; Insurability.

The City shall deliver marketable and insurable fee simple absolute title to the Property, free and clear of all liens, encumbrances, restrictions, reversionary interests, rights of re-entry, possibilities of reverter, defeasible conditions, and other title defects or exceptions, except Permitted Exceptions. The City shall provide a standard owner's title insurance policy in the amount of the purchase price. The Property conveyed to Developer shall not be subject to any reversionary interest, right of re-entry, possibility of reverter, fee simple determinable, fee simple subject to condition subsequent, or any other form of defeasible fee estate in favor of the City or any other party.

[Drafting Note — Conflict C1 (reversionary interest / defeasible fee). This Section 4.4, together with Article 14, implements the Construction Lender's absolute requirement (Loan Term Sheet Sections 4.2, 7.1(1), 12(a)) that the Property not be subject to any reversionary or defeasible interest. The Construction Lender states that any such interest would render the title uninsurable, subordinate the mortgage to the City's right to reclaim the property, prevent foreclosure, and render the Construction Loan unsecurable — and that incorporation of Resolution Condition #8(h)'s reversionary interest "as stated in the Resolution, would make the Loan impossible to close." Resolution Condition #8(h), however, requires Developer to grant the City a reversionary interest with automatic reversion if Phase I is not Substantially Complete within 36 months of conveyance. This is the central cross-document conflict. Article 14 substitutes an alternative completion-assurance mechanism (a contractual repurchase option plus enhanced performance security) that is intended to satisfy the City's policy objective of ensuring timely Phase I completion while preserving Developer's fee simple absolute title and the Construction Lender's first-priority security interest. The City must agree to deviate from the literal terms of Resolution Condition #8(h); this requires either (i) a further City Council action amending or superseding Condition #8(h), or (ii) a determination by Corporation Counsel that the alternative mechanism satisfies the condition. This is the single most important open item and must be resolved before execution.]

[Drafting Note — Conflict C2 (36-month reversion clock vs. 30-month construction). Even setting aside the title-insurability problem, Resolution Condition #8(h) measures the 36-month reversion period from the date of conveyance, while Phase I Substantial Completion is targeted for September 30, 2028 with groundbreaking no later than March 1, 2026. If conveyance occurs in mid-2025 (the Term Sheet's target closing date), the 36-month period could expire before the Phase I Substantial Completion deadline, meaning the reversion could be triggered through no fault of Developer. This timing mismatch further supports substituting an alternative mechanism measured from groundbreaking (or aligned to the Substantial Completion deadline plus cure and Force Majeure extensions).]

### Section 4.5 Closing.

Closing shall occur within thirty (30) days following satisfaction or waiver of all conditions precedent to closing. The parties' target closing date is mid-2025, subject to the timing of rezoning, IEPA approvals, and satisfaction of other conditions.

## ARTICLE 5. PUBLIC INCENTIVES

### Section 5.1 Land Write-Down.

The City conveys the Property at 50% of appraised as-is market value, resulting in a land write-down incentive of $9,200,000. The land write-down shall constitute a separate public incentive and shall not be charged against, or counted toward, the $48,000,000 TIF reimbursement cap set forth in Section 5.2.

[Drafting Note — Conflict C11 (land write-down vs. TIF cap). The TIF Memo (Section VII) notes that the land write-down is itself an eligible TIF cost and that, if the City records it as a TIF expenditure from the Special Tax Allocation Fund, it counts against the $48,000,000 cap — reducing the reimbursement available to Developer. The TIF Memo recommends the development agreement "clearly specify whether the land write-down is charged against the TIF cap or constitutes a separate incentive funded from another source." Developer's position, reflected in Section 5.1, is that the land write-down is a separate incentive funded from the City's general/capital sources and is not charged against the TIF cap. The City should confirm the funding source. This is an open item.]

### Section 5.2 Tax Increment Financing.

(a) **Pledge.** The City shall pledge up to $48,000,000 in TIF increment over the remaining life of the TIF District to reimburse Developer for eligible TIF costs incurred in connection with the Project. Eligible TIF costs shall include, without limitation: public infrastructure improvements, environmental remediation costs, parking structure construction costs, the public plaza and community center, and other eligible project costs under the TIF Act and the Lakemont Central TIF District Redevelopment Plan and Project adopted by Ordinance No. 2019-42.

(b) **Pay-As-You-Go.** TIF reimbursement shall be on a pay-as-you-go basis. Developer shall front all eligible costs and shall be reimbursed from available TIF increment as generated by the Project and other developments within the TIF District. The City shall not issue TIF bonds or notes for the Project.

(c) **No Guarantee; Shortfall Not a Default.** TIF reimbursement is subject to the availability of increment. The City does not guarantee that the full $48,000,000 cap will be reached. A shortfall in TIF reimbursement, or the failure of cumulative increment to reach the $48,000,000 cap by the TIF District expiration, shall not constitute a default by the City or by Developer under this Agreement, and shall not give either party a right to terminate this Agreement.

(d) **Priority of Reimbursement.** TIF reimbursements shall be made in the following priority order: (i) first, environmental remediation costs for Parcels 12-24-300-016 and 12-24-300-017; (ii) second, public infrastructure costs, including the City's off-site infrastructure commitment and Developer's eligible on-site infrastructure expenditures; (iii) third, the Phase IV public parking structure, public plaza, and community center costs; and (iv) fourth, all other eligible costs, including professional services and financing costs.

(e) **Good-Faith Effort to Seek Extension.** If, as of December 31, 2038 (the "TIF Benchmark Date"), cumulative TIF reimbursement to Developer is tracking materially below the projections supporting the $48,000,000 cap, the City shall use good-faith efforts to explore and, if feasible, pursue an extension of the TIF District or the adoption of a successor TIF district, subject to applicable law and the availability of eligibility findings. The City's obligation under this subsection is one of good-faith effort and does not guarantee any extension.

(f) **Reimbursement Mechanics.** Developer shall submit periodic reimbursement requests accompanied by certified cost documentation prepared by Clearmont Accounting Group LLP (or another nationally recognized accounting firm acceptable to the City). The City shall process reimbursement requests within sixty (60) days of receipt of complete documentation. The City shall have audit rights with respect to reimbursement claims upon reasonable notice. Interest shall accrue on undisputed reimbursement amounts not paid within ninety (90) days of a complete request at the rate of [__]% per annum.

[Drafting Note — Conflict C10 (TIF shortfall risk). The Term Sheet (Section 5.2) provides that TIF reimbursement is subject to availability of increment and that the City does not guarantee the full $48,000,000 cap. The TIF Memo (Section IV) concludes that, adjusted for the Phase I property tax abatement, cumulative increment through 2042 may be approximately $38,000,000 to $42,000,000 — potentially $6,000,000 to $10,000,000 short of the $48,000,000 cap — and that under a worst-case timing scenario (maximum permitted delays) the cap is unlikely to be reached. The TIF Memo recommends that the development agreement (i) make TIF reimbursement expressly contingent on actual increment collected, (ii) establish a reimbursement priority, (iii) include a good-faith extension covenant, and (iv) expressly state that a TIF shortfall does not constitute a City default. Sections 5.2(c), (d), and (e) implement these recommendations. From the Developer's perspective, the priority structure (remediation and infrastructure first) protects the most critical reimbursements, and the good-faith extension covenant and interest-on-delay provision mitigate timing risk. Developer should nonetheless recognize that the TIF Memo (City counsel's analysis) indicates material shortfall risk; the Developer bears the timing and shortfall risk under the pay-as-you-go structure. The Lakeshore Side Letter (Section 4.3(a)) requires Investor consent to any change to the TIF reimbursement cap or eligible cost categories, and Section 3.5 prohibits Developer from agreeing to terms materially impairing the 14% Minimum IRR without Investor consent — so the TIF structure is also constrained by Developer's internal investor obligations.]

[Drafting Note — Open item. The interest rate in Section 5.2(f) and the precise documentation/audit procedures should be finalized. The TIF Memo (Section VIII.6) recommends certified cost documentation by Clearmont Accounting Group LLP and City audit rights.]

### Section 5.3 Infrastructure Cost-Sharing.

(a) **City Off-Site Commitment.** The City shall fund $12,500,000 for off-site infrastructure improvements, including: (i) widening of Lakemont Station Boulevard between Oak Street and Maple Avenue; (ii) installation of a new traffic signal at the Oak Street/Lakemont Station Boulevard intersection; (iii) extension of the water main along Lakemont Station Boulevard; and (iv) sanitary sewer upgrade and capacity expansion. The City's off-site infrastructure funding shall be appropriated and disbursed according to a schedule set forth in Exhibit [__], coordinated with the phased construction timeline, and shall be funded from the City's general revenue or capital improvement funds, not from the TIF Special Tax Allocation Fund.

(b) **Developer On-Site Responsibility.** Developer shall fund $8,300,000 for on-site infrastructure improvements, including internal roadways, utilities, stormwater management, pedestrian pathways, and landscape improvements within the Property, to the extent such improvements serve a documented public purpose and are eligible for TIF reimbursement.

(c) **Total Infrastructure Budget.** The total combined infrastructure budget is $20,800,000 ($12,500,000 City + $8,300,000 Developer).

[Drafting Note — Conflict C12 (City infrastructure funding source vs. TIF cap). The TIF Memo (Section VII) notes that, if the City's $12,500,000 infrastructure commitment is funded from TIF, it counts against the $48,000,000 cap — reducing reimbursement available to Developer. The TIF Memo recommends the funding source be specified in the development agreement. Developer's position, reflected in Section 5.3(a), is that the City's off-site infrastructure commitment is funded from general revenue or capital improvement funds, not TIF, preserving the full $48,000,000 cap for Developer reimbursement. The City should confirm the funding source. This is an open item.]

### Section 5.4 Property Tax Abatement.

The City shall provide a twelve (12)-year declining property tax abatement on Phase I improvements only, as follows: Years 1–4 — 75% abatement; Years 5–8 — 50% abatement; Years 9–12 — 25% abatement; Year 13 and thereafter — 0% (full assessment). The abatement shall apply to the incremental increase in assessed value attributable to the Phase I improvements, measured from the pre-development base equalized assessed value. No property tax abatement is provided for Phase II, Phase III, or Phase IV under this Agreement.

[Drafting Note — Conflict C28 (abatement–TIF interaction; later-phase abatement). The TIF Memo (Sections IV.B, VII) identifies an internal tension: the Phase I abatement benefits Developer by reducing its property tax burden but simultaneously reduces the TIF increment available for Developer reimbursement (because the abatement reduces the assessed value of Phase I improvements during the abatement years). The TIF Memo recommends the development agreement acknowledge this interaction and consider structuring the abatement to minimize its impact on TIF-capturable increment, if legally permissible. Developer should consider whether to negotiate an abatement structure that applies only to the non-TIF-captured portion of the tax bill. Additionally, Term Sheet Section 18.4 states that no abatement is provided for later phases and that the parties' positions on later-phase incentives "shall be addressed during negotiation of the definitive Agreement." Developer may seek abatement or additional incentives for later phases; this is an open item. The Lakeshore Side Letter (Section 4.3(b)) requires Investor consent to any change to the abatement schedule.]

### Section 5.5 Building Permit Fee Waiver.

The City shall waive building permit fees for Phase I and Phase II, with an estimated combined value of $1,400,000 ($920,000 for Phase I and $480,000 for Phase II). No building permit fee waiver is contemplated for Phase III or Phase IV. The permit fee waivers shall constitute a separate incentive and shall not be charged against the $48,000,000 TIF reimbursement cap.

### Section 5.6 Summary of Public Incentives.

The public incentives are summarized as follows: land write-down $9,200,000; TIF reimbursement up to $48,000,000; City off-site infrastructure funding $12,500,000; Phase I property tax abatement (12-year declining); and permit fee waivers $1,400,000.

## ARTICLE 6. ENVIRONMENTAL MATTERS

### Section 6.1 Environmental Conditions.

The environmental conditions of the Property are as documented in the Phase II ESA prepared by Greenfield Environmental Consultants LLC, dated November 8, 2024, and are summarized as follows:

(a) **Parcel 12-24-300-015.** No environmental contamination requiring further action was identified; no further investigation or remediation is required.

(b) **Parcel 12-24-300-016.** BTEX contamination in soil and groundwater over approximately 1.8 acres, and localized TCE contamination, attributable to the former Lakemont Ironworks operations (former UST farm and metal finishing/degreasing operations). Remediation under the IEPA VSRP is required, at an estimated cost of $2,800,000 and an estimated timeline of fourteen (14) to eighteen (18) months, culminating in issuance of an NFR Letter.

(c) **Parcel 12-24-300-017.** Low-level metals contamination (primarily arsenic) in shallow fill material, suitable for residential use with installation of an engineered barrier (clean fill cap), at an estimated cost of $340,000.

The total estimated environmental remediation cost is $3,140,000.

### Section 6.2 City Indemnification of Pre-Existing Environmental Conditions.

The City shall indemnify, defend, and hold harmless Developer, its members, officers, employees, agents, contractors, and successors from and against all claims, demands, actions, losses, costs, damages, liabilities, and expenses (including reasonable attorneys' fees and court costs) arising from or related to pre-existing environmental conditions on the Property (Parcels 12-24-300-015, 12-24-300-016, and 12-24-300-017), including without limitation third-party claims, governmental enforcement actions, contribution or cost-recovery actions under federal or state environmental laws, and any contamination attributable to the former Lakemont Ironworks operations that ceased in 2009. The contamination identified in the Phase II ESA is pre-existing and attributable to the former Lakemont Ironworks operations; the City acquired the affected parcels with knowledge of the environmental conditions. This indemnity shall survive the expiration or termination of this Agreement and the conveyance of the Property.

### Section 6.3 Remediation Responsibility and Cost Allocation.

(a) **Performance.** Developer shall perform, or contract for, the remediation of Parcel 12-24-300-016 under the VSRP and the engineered barrier installation on Parcel 12-24-300-017, using Greenfield Environmental Consultants LLC or another qualified environmental consultant. Developer shall be the named applicant on the VSRP enrollment and NFR Letter application for Parcel 12-24-300-016, except as the parties may otherwise agree.

(b) **Base Cost.** The base estimated environmental remediation cost is $3,140,000 ($2,800,000 for Parcel 016 and $340,000 for Parcel 017). Environmental remediation costs are eligible TIF costs and shall be reimbursed to Developer on a first-priority basis under Section 5.2(d).

(c) **Cost Overruns.** The Phase II ESA (Section 5.3) warns that actual remediation costs may exceed the base estimate by 25% to 35% (with total Parcel 016 cost exposure of approximately $3,500,000 to $3,800,000, and combined total exposure of approximately $3,800,000 to $4,200,000 in a significant-scope-expansion scenario), due to potential discovery of additional contamination, extended groundwater treatment, or supplemental regulatory requirements. Any environmental remediation cost exceeding the base estimate of $3,140,000 shall be borne by the City, or, at Developer's election, shall be reimbursed to Developer as an eligible TIF cost on a first-priority basis. The City shall not be entitled to offset such overruns against any other incentive or against the purchase price.

(d) **Vapor Intrusion.** If the vapor intrusion screening evaluation recommended in the Phase II ESA (Sections 7.1, 8.1) indicates that vapor mitigation measures (such as sub-slab depressurization systems, vapor barriers, or passive venting systems) are warranted for structures on or adjacent to the contaminated area of Parcel 12-24-300-016, the cost of such vapor mitigation measures shall be borne by the City or reimbursed to Developer as an eligible TIF cost, as a pre-existing environmental condition.

(e) **Off-Site Investigation.** If IEPA requires off-site groundwater monitoring (including on or adjacent to the Lakemont Central Metra station property, as recommended in the Phase II ESA Section 8.3), the City shall assist Developer in obtaining any required access from Metra or other third parties, and the cost of such off-site investigation shall be borne by the City or reimbursed as an eligible TIF cost.

[Drafting Note — Conflict C14 (environmental cost allocation / overruns). The Term Sheet (Section 6) provides that the City indemnifies Developer for pre-existing environmental conditions and that remediation costs are TIF-eligible, but is silent on (i) which party performs the remediation, (ii) who bears cost overruns, and (iii) vapor mitigation and off-site investigation costs. The Phase II ESA (Section 8.2) recommends the transactional documents clearly identify the responsible party, the cost-allocation mechanism, and a cost-overrun provision (recommending 25–35% contingency), and notes that the City bears primary responsible-party status under the Illinois Environmental Protection Act because the contamination is pre-existing and the City owned the property with knowledge of the conditions. The Lakeshore Side Letter (Section 4.3(d)) requires Investor consent for any acceptance of environmental remediation obligations exceeding $3,140,000 without a corresponding increase in TIF reimbursement or direct City funding sufficient to offset the excess. Sections 6.3(c), (d), and (e) allocate overruns, vapor mitigation, and off-site investigation costs to the City (or as first-priority TIF costs), consistent with the pre-existing nature of the contamination and the City's indemnity. The City may resist bearing overruns directly; this is a key open negotiation item. Developer's internal constraint: any allocation of environmental obligations above $3,140,000 to Developer (rather than the City or TIF) requires Lakeshore's consent.]

The consolidation plat shall expressly reference the institutional control area by legal description.

### Section 6.5 Environmental Insurance.

Developer shall obtain pollution legal liability insurance in an amount not less than $10,000,000, naming the City and the Construction Lender as additional insureds, with a policy term covering the full construction period and a three-year extended reporting period, in form acceptable to the Construction Lender.

## ARTICLE 9. DEVELOPMENT OBLIGATIONS

### Section 9.1 Construction Standards.

Developer shall construct the Project in accordance with plans and specifications approved under Section 3.9, the Project Budget, and all applicable laws, codes, and ordinances. Developer shall maintain a construction budget that includes a minimum 5% hard-cost contingency for each Phase.

### Section 9.2 LEED Gold Certification.

All buildings constructed as part of the Project shall achieve LEED Gold certification, or an equivalent green building certification, as administered by the U.S. Green Building Council. Developer shall register each building with the USGBC prior to commencement of construction of the applicable building and shall submit documentation of certification status to the City upon receipt of certification. The determination of whether an alternative certification is "equivalent" shall be made against objective green-building standards and shall not be subject to any single official's sole discretion.

[Drafting Note — Conflict C20 (LEED "or equivalent" acceptability). Resolution Condition #8(f) provides that LEED Gold "or an equivalent green building certification as determined acceptable by the Director of the Community Development Department, Paul R. Vasquez" satisfies the requirement. The Term Sheet (Section 11.2) provides for LEED Gold "or an equivalent green building certification as may be approved by the City in writing." The Loan Term Sheet (Section 6.1) requires LEED Gold "or an equivalent green building standard." Developer's position, reflected in Section 9.2, is that equivalence should be determined against objective standards rather than a single official's sole discretion, to avoid an unreasonable veto. The City may insist on the Resolution's Director-determination language; this is an open item.]

### Section 9.3 Prevailing Wage.

(a) All construction work performed pursuant to this Agreement that constitutes "public works" within the meaning of the Illinois Prevailing Wage Act, 820 ILCS 130/0.01 et seq. (the "Prevailing Wage Act"), shall be subject to the prevailing wage requirements of the Prevailing Wage Act. This includes, without limitation, the off-site public infrastructure improvements, the on-site infrastructure improvements to the extent TIF-reimbursed, the environmental remediation to the extent TIF-reimbursed, and the Phase IV public parking structure, public plaza, and community center.

(b) Developer shall include prevailing wage requirements in all contracts and subcontracts for construction work subject to the Prevailing Wage Act, shall maintain certified payroll records and make them available to the City and the Illinois Department of Labor upon request, and shall post prevailing wage rate schedules at the construction site.

(c) The parties acknowledge that the scope of Prevailing Wage Act applicability to purely private components of the Project (including market-rate residential, private office, retail, and hotel construction) is a matter of statutory interpretation that may be debatable. The Term Sheet (Section 11.1) states that "all construction performed on the Project shall comply with the Illinois Prevailing Wage Act." To the extent the parties agree to extend prevailing wage coverage beyond the statutorily-required scope as a contractual matter, such extension shall be expressly set forth in Exhibit [__] and Developer shall be entitled to seek corresponding adjustments to the public incentives to offset any incremental cost.

[Drafting Note — Conflict C13 (prevailing wage scope). The Term Sheet (Section 11.1) states that "all construction performed on the Project shall comply with the Illinois Prevailing Wage Act." The TIF Memo (Section V) concludes that, given the substantial public financial assistance (land write-down, TIF, abatement, fee waivers), the Prevailing Wage Act "likely applies to all construction activity on the project site, not merely the TIF-reimbursed components," and recommends the development agreement specify the scope with precision. The TIF Memo notes that the Term Sheet's "all construction" language "converts a potentially debatable statutory question into an unambiguous contractual obligation" and that the Developer "should understand that this provision may increase construction costs for components... that might otherwise be argued to fall outside the statutory mandate." Developer's position, reflected in Section 9.3, is to (i) comply with the Prevailing Wage Act where it applies by statute, (ii) flag the debatability of full-project coverage, and (iii) seek offsetting incentive adjustments if the parties contractually extend coverage to purely private components. The City's strongest position (per the TIF Memo) is broad application. This is a significant cost open item; the Lakeshore Side Letter (Section 3.5) prohibits Developer from agreeing to terms materially increasing total Project costs beyond $412,000,000 without Investor consent, so any contractual extension of prevailing wage that materially increases cost may require Lakeshore consent.]

### Section 9.4 Insurance.

Developer shall maintain, or cause its general contractor to maintain, throughout the construction period for each Phase, the following minimum insurance coverages: (a) Commercial General Liability (CGL) — $10,000,000 per occurrence and $25,000,000 in the aggregate; (b) Umbrella/Excess Liability — $25,000,000; (c) Builder's Risk — full replacement value of each Phase under construction; (d) Workers' Compensation — statutory limits; (e) Automobile Liability — $5,000,000 combined single limit; and (f) Professional Liability (Architect/Engineer) — $5,000,000. The City and the Construction Lender shall be named as additional insureds/loss payees, as applicable. All carriers shall be rated A- VII or better by A.M. Best. All policies shall provide not less than 30 days' prior written notice of cancellation or material modification.

[Drafting Note — Conflict C9 (CGL aggregate). The Term Sheet (Section 11.3) requires CGL coverage of $10,000,000 per occurrence and $20,000,000 in the aggregate. The Loan Term Sheet (Section 5) requires CGL coverage of $10,000,000 per occurrence and $25,000,000 in the aggregate. Section 9.4 adopts the higher aggregate ($25,000,000) required by the Construction Lender, which also satisfies the Term Sheet. The Term Sheet's lower aggregate is superseded by the Construction Lender's requirement. Confirm the City accepts the $25,000,000 aggregate.]

### Section 9.5 Construction Monitoring.

Developer shall submit monthly draw requests supported by AIA G702/G703 documentation to the Construction Lender and shall provide quarterly construction progress reports to the City's Community Development Department, including percentage of completion, updated schedule, and identification of any delays, within thirty (30) days following the end of each calendar quarter during which active construction is occurring.

[Drafting Note — Conflict C19 (local hiring — goal vs. requirement). Resolution Condition #8(c)(i), however, refers to "a local hiring goal requiring that not less than thirty-five percent (35%)." Developer's position, reflected in Section 10.1, is that the 35% commitment is a good-faith goal, not a hard requirement, and that missing the goal is not a default if good-faith efforts are demonstrated. This is a new obligation introduced by the Resolution. Developer's position, reflected in Section 10.2, is to (i) limit the obligation to a lease/operating-agreement covenant enforced through commercially reasonable efforts (rather than direct Developer liability for tenant wages), (ii) index the rate to avoid a fixed nominal amount eroding over time, and (iii) limit scope to retail and hotel permanent employees as stated in the Resolution. ### Section 10.4 Community Benefits Agreement.

## ARTICLE 11. AFFORDABLE HOUSING

### Section 11.1 Affordable Units.

Developer shall include 106 Affordable Units across the Project, restricted to households earning at or below 60% of AMI, representing 20% of the 530 total residential units. The Affordable Units shall be distributed as follows: 64 Affordable Units in Phase I (out of 320 total residential units) and 42 Affordable Units in Phase III (out of 210 total residential units).

### Section 11.2 Integration and Comparability.

Affordable Units shall be distributed proportionally across unit types (studio, one-bedroom, and two-bedroom) and shall not be segregated or concentrated on particular floors or in separate wings. Affordable Units shall be comparable in design, finishes, and amenity access to market-rate units within the same building, subject to reasonable variations consistent with industry practice.

### Section 11.3 Affordability Restriction Period.

Affordability restrictions shall remain in effect for a minimum of thirty (30) years from the date of issuance of the certificate of occupancy for the applicable Phase. The affordability restrictions shall be memorialized by a restrictive covenant recorded against the applicable parcel and running with the land, in a form to be set forth in Exhibit [__].

### Section 11.4 Compliance.

Developer shall comply with the City's Affordable Housing Compliance Program and shall provide annual income certification documentation to the City's Community Development Department for all Affordable Units throughout the affordability restriction period.

The affordability covenant form (open item) should be developed in coordination with the Construction Lender and title company to ensure it does not impair title insurability or the Construction Lender's security.]

## ARTICLE 12. PHASING, TIMELINES, AND FORCE MAJEURE

### Section 12.1 Phasing Schedule.

The Project shall be constructed in four Phases in accordance with the following schedule:

| Phase | Commencement Deadline | Substantial Completion Deadline |
|---|---|---|
| Phase I | Groundbreaking no later than March 1, 2026 | September 30, 2028 |
| Phase II | Within 12 months of Phase I Substantial Completion | Within 24 months of Phase II commencement |
| Phase III | Within 12 months of Phase II Substantial Completion | Within 30 months of Phase III commencement |
| Phase IV | Concurrent with Phase III commencement | Within 24 months of Phase IV commencement |

The commencement deadline for each subsequent Phase is measured from the Substantial Completion date of the immediately preceding Phase, as may be extended by Force Majeure. Developer shall provide quarterly construction progress reports to the City.

### Section 12.2 Substantial Completion.

"Substantial Completion" of a Phase means the completion of construction of the applicable Phase to the point where the building or improvements can be occupied and used for their intended purpose, as evidenced by the issuance of a temporary or final certificate of occupancy by the City's Building Department for the applicable building(s). Substantial Completion of a Phase shall not be delayed by punch-list items, cosmetic defects, or minor incomplete work that does not prevent occupancy and use of the building for its intended purpose.

[Drafting Note — Open item O8 (Substantial Completion definition). The Term Sheet (Section 13) states that Substantial Completion "shall be defined in the definitive Agreement" and generally means completion to the point of occupancy, evidenced by a temporary or final certificate of occupancy. Resolution Condition #8(h) references Substantial Completion "as such term shall be defined in the Development Agreement." Section 12.2 defines Substantial Completion in Developer-favorable terms tied to certificate of occupancy issuance and excluding punch-list/cosmetic items. The City may seek a stricter definition; reconcile.]

### Section 12.3 Force Majeure.

(a) **Definition.** "Force Majeure" means acts of God, fire, flood, earthquake, hurricane, tornado, severe weather events, pandemic, epidemic, quarantine, governmental health orders, war, terrorism, civil unrest, insurrection, strikes or labor disputes (other than those caused by Developer's labor practices or failure to comply with applicable labor laws), industry-wide supply chain disruptions beyond Developer's reasonable control, governmental moratoria on construction, actions or inactions of governmental authorities (including IEPA delays in reviewing or approving the RAP or NFR Letter application, and delays in the rezoning or parcel consolidation process), and actions or inactions of the City of Lakemont that prevent or materially delay construction.

(b) **Effect.** Force Majeure events shall extend the applicable deadlines for commencement and Substantial Completion on a day-for-day basis for the duration of the Force Majeure event.

(c) **Aggregate Cap.** The maximum extension of time available under this Section 12.3 shall not exceed eighteen (18) months in the aggregate across the Project.

(d) **Notice.** Developer shall provide written notice to the City within fifteen (15) business days of the occurrence of a Force Majeure event, describing the nature, anticipated duration, and expected impact, with updated notices as circumstances change.

(e) **Mitigation.** Developer shall use commercially reasonable efforts to mitigate the effects of any Force Majeure event.

(f) **Exclusions.** Force Majeure shall not include financial inability to perform, changes in market conditions, increases in the cost of labor or materials (unless attributable to an industry-wide supply chain disruption), Developer's failure to obtain financing, or any event caused by Developer's negligence or willful misconduct.

[Drafting Note — Conflict C21 (force majeure cap). The Term Sheet (Section 14) provides an 18-month aggregate Force Majeure cap across the Project. The Loan Term Sheet (Section 9(9)) provides Force Majeure extensions of "up to 180 days" (six months) for the Phase I Substantial Completion default, plus any Construction Lender-approved extension periods under Section 2.3. There is a gap: the Agreement permits up to 18 months of Force Majeure relief, but the Construction Loan may default after only 180 days (six months) of Force Majeure delay for Phase I. Developer should negotiate with the Construction Lender to align the loan's Force Majeure cap with the Agreement's 18-month cap, or to obtain Construction Lender extension approvals as needed. This is an open item. Section 12.3(a) also expressly includes IEPA review delays and rezoning/consolidation delays as Force Majeure (addressing the Phase II ESA's recommendation, Section 8.2(d), that the transactional documents address whether delay in NFR Letter issuance constitutes Force Majeure or a deadline extension).]

### Section 12.4 Schedule Buffer.

Developer maintains a target accelerated schedule approximately two years ahead of the contractual maximum deadlines set forth in Section 12.1. The contractual maximum deadlines govern for purposes of this Agreement and provide buffer for regulatory, market, and construction contingencies.

## ARTICLE 13. DEFAULT AND CURE; TERMINATION

### Section 13.1 Monetary Defaults.

In the event of a monetary default by either party (including Developer's failure to pay the land purchase price or the City's failure to fund committed infrastructure amounts), the non-defaulting party shall provide written notice specifying the nature and amount of the default. The defaulting party shall have sixty (60) days from receipt of such notice to cure the default.

### Section 13.2 Non-Monetary Defaults.

In the event of a non-monetary default by either party (including failure to commence construction within applicable deadlines, failure to comply with affordable housing requirements, or failure to maintain required insurance), the non-defaulting party shall provide written notice specifying the nature of the default in reasonable detail. The defaulting party shall have ninety (90) days from receipt of such notice to cure the default; provided, that if the nature of the default is such that it cannot reasonably be cured within ninety (90) days, the defaulting party shall have such additional time as is reasonably necessary to effect a cure, so long as the defaulting party has commenced cure efforts within the initial 90-day period and is diligently and continuously pursuing cure to completion.

### Section 13.3 Termination by City.

The City may terminate this Agreement upon: (a) a material breach by Developer that remains uncured after expiration of the applicable cure period (as extended by the Construction Lender's cure rights under Article 16); (b) Developer's failure to commence Phase I construction within six (6) months following land conveyance; (c) Developer's abandonment of the Project, evidenced by cessation of all construction activity for a period of one hundred eighty (180) consecutive days (other than cessation caused by Force Majeure); and (d) such other events of default as specified in this Agreement. The City's termination right is subject to the completion-assurance mechanism in Article 14 and the Construction Lender's cure rights in Article 16, and shall not include any reversionary interest or automatic reversion of title.

### Section 13.4 Termination by Developer.

Developer may terminate this Agreement upon: (a) the City's failure to deliver clean, marketable, and insurable fee simple title to the Property at closing in accordance with Article 4; (b) the City's failure to fund its committed infrastructure obligations ($12,500,000) within the timelines specified in Exhibit [__]; (c) a condemnation or taking by eminent domain (by any entity, including the City) of more than 15% of the Property by area or value; (d) the City's failure to complete the rezoning or parcel consolidation within the timelines agreed by the parties; (e) the City's material breach that remains uncured after the applicable cure period; and (f) such other events of default as specified in this Agreement.

[Drafting Note — Conflict C23 (condemnation 15%). The Term Sheet (Section 15) permits Developer to terminate if a condemnation by "any entity other than the City" exceeds 15% of the Property. The Loan Term Sheet (Section 9(8)) treats condemnation of more than 15% of the Project site by area or value as an Event of Default under the loan. Section 13.4(c) extends Developer's termination right to condemnation by any entity, including the City, to align with the Loan Term Sheet's broader treatment and to protect Developer against a City-initiated taking. The City may resist including City condemnation; reconcile. The 15% threshold is consistent across documents.]

### Section 13.5 Remedies.

Upon termination by a non-defaulting party, the non-defaulting party shall have all remedies available at law or in equity, including damages, specific performance, and injunctive relief, subject to the dispute resolution provisions of Article 18 and the completion-assurance mechanism in Article 14. Neither party shall be liable for consequential, special, or punitive damages except as expressly provided in this Agreement.

## ARTICLE 14. COMPLETION ASSURANCE (ALTERNATIVE TO REVERSIONARY INTEREST)

### Section 14.1 No Reversionary Interest.

The parties acknowledge that the Construction Lender has advised that any reversionary interest, right of re-entry, possibility of reverter, or other defeasible fee estate in favor of the City would render Developer's fee simple title uninsurable, subordinate the Construction Lender's first-priority mortgage, prevent foreclosure, and render the Construction Loan unsecurable. Accordingly, notwithstanding Resolution Condition #8(h), the Property shall not be subject to any reversionary interest, right of re-entry, possibility of reverter, fee simple determinable, fee simple subject to condition subsequent, or any other form of defeasible fee estate in favor of the City or any other party. Developer's fee simple title to the Property shall remain absolute and indefeasible, subject only to Permitted Exceptions.

### Section 14.2 Alternative Completion-Assurance Mechanism.

In lieu of the reversionary interest contemplated by Resolution Condition #8(h), the parties adopt the following alternative mechanism to satisfy the City's policy objective of ensuring timely Phase I completion while preserving Developer's fee simple absolute title and the Construction Lender's first-priority security interest:

(a) **Phase I Completion Deadline.** Developer shall achieve Substantial Completion of Phase I by September 30, 2028, as such deadline may be extended by Force Majeure under Section 12.3 and by the Construction Lender-approved extension periods.

(b) **Cure Periods.** If Developer fails to achieve Phase I Substantial Completion by the deadline (as extended), the City shall provide written notice of such failure. Developer shall have the cure periods set forth in Section 13.2 (90 days, plus additional time if diligently pursuing cure), followed by the Construction Lender's additional cure rights under Article 16 (not less than 60 days beyond Developer's cure period for non-monetary defaults, with extension if the Construction Lender is diligently pursuing a cure, including time necessary to obtain possession through foreclosure or a receiver).

(c) **Contractual Repurchase Option.** If, after expiration of all cure periods (including the Construction Lender's extended cure period), Developer has failed to achieve Phase I Substantial Completion and the Construction Lender has not cured or completed Phase I, the City shall have a contractual option to repurchase the Property, exercisable only upon (i) Developer's failure to achieve Phase I Substantial Completion within the agreed timeframe, (ii) expiration of all cure periods (including the Construction Lender's extended cure period), and (iii) the Construction Lender's prior written consent or full satisfaction of the outstanding Construction Loan balance. The repurchase price shall be the lesser of (A) the original purchase price paid by Developer ($9,200,000) or (B) the then-current fair market value of the Property as determined by an independent MAI appraiser mutually selected by the parties. The repurchase option shall be subordinate to the Construction Lender's mortgage and shall not be exercisable until the Construction Lender's mortgage is satisfied or the Construction Lender consents.

(d) **Enhanced Performance Security.** The $15,000,000 Phase I performance security under Article 8 shall be available to the City as liquidated security for Developer's Phase I completion obligation, in addition to the repurchase option. The performance security shall be the City's primary financial remedy for a Phase I completion failure, to be drawn upon only after expiration of all cure periods.

(e) **No Automatic Reversion.** Title to the Property shall not automatically revert to the City under any circumstance. The City's remedies for a Phase I completion failure are limited to the contractual remedies set forth in this Article 14 (the repurchase option and the performance security) and the termination and damage remedies under Article 13, in each case subject to the Construction Lender's rights under Article 16.

[Drafting Note — Conflict C1 (reversionary interest — central conflict). This Article 14 is the most critical provision in this draft and the single most important open item. Resolution Condition #8(h) requires Developer to grant the City a reversionary interest with automatic reversion of fee simple title if Phase I is not Substantially Complete within 36 months of conveyance. The Construction Lender (Loan Term Sheet Sections 4.2, 7.1(1), 12(a), and the "Specific Concern" discussion in Section 12) states unequivocally that it "cannot commit to a loan secured by a mortgage on property that is subject to a reversionary interest or any form of defeasible fee estate" and that incorporation of the reversionary interest "as stated in the Resolution, would make the Loan impossible to close." The Construction Lender suggests five alternative mechanisms: (i) a contractual repurchase option exercisable only after cure periods and Construction Lender consent/satisfaction; (ii) a performance escrow/additional letter of credit; (iii) liquidated damages; (iv) an enhanced completion guaranty; and (v) a right of first refusal subordinate to the mortgage. Section 14.2 adopts a combination of alternatives (i) and (ii) (repurchase option plus the existing $15,000,000 performance security), which the Construction Lender specifically identified as acceptable. The Lakeshore Side Letter (Section 7(d)) requires Investor consent to any clawback, reversionary interest, right of re-entry, repurchase option, or similar mechanism, and acknowledges that the parties "must negotiate terms compatible with the Construction Lender's requirements and Investor's interests." Accordingly, this Article 14 requires both City agreement (to deviate from the literal terms of Resolution Condition #8(h), likely via a further City Council action or a Corporation Counsel determination that the alternative satisfies the condition) and Lakeshore consent. This must be resolved before execution; if the City insists on the literal reversionary interest, the Construction Loan cannot close and the Project financing fails.]

[Drafting Note — Conflict C2 (36-month clock). Resolution Condition #8(h) measures the 36-month period from the date of conveyance. Section 14.2(a) instead measures the completion obligation against the Phase I Substantial Completion deadline of September 30, 2028 (as extended by Force Majeure and Construction Lender extensions), which is tied to the March 1, 2026 groundbreaking. This avoids the timing mismatch under which a 36-month-from-conveyance clock could expire before the Substantial Completion deadline. The City should confirm acceptance of measuring the completion assurance against the Substantial Completion deadline rather than a fixed 36-month-from-conveyance period.]

## ARTICLE 15. ASSIGNMENT AND CHANGE OF CONTROL

### Section 15.1 Restriction on Assignment.

Developer may not assign this Agreement, or any interest therein, in whole or in part, without the prior written consent of the City, which consent may be withheld in the City's reasonable discretion; provided, that the City's consent shall not be required, and shall not be unreasonably withheld, conditioned, or delayed, for the assignments described in Sections 15.1.1, 15.1.2, and 15.1.3. Any purported assignment in violation of this Article 15 shall be void and of no force and effect and shall constitute a material default. For purposes of this Article 15, "assignment" includes any direct or indirect transfer, conveyance, pledge, hypothecation, or other disposition of this Agreement or any interest therein, whether voluntary or involuntary, by operation of law or otherwise.

### Section 15.1.1 Permitted Assignment to Pellegrini-Controlled Entity.

Developer may assign this Agreement without the City's prior written consent to an entity in which Marcus J. Pellegrini (or the Pellegrini Family Trust) holds at least a 50% controlling interest and which entity expressly assumes in writing all of Developer's obligations under this Agreement. Developer shall provide the City with written notice of any such permitted assignment, together with a copy of the assignment and assumption agreement, within fifteen (15) days of the effective date.

### Section 15.1.2 Permitted Transfers Among Existing Members.

Transfers of membership interests in Developer among the existing members (Pellegrini Family Trust, Lakeshore Capital Partners Fund III LP, and TransitWorks Equity Group LLC), and transfers by Marcus J. Pellegrini for estate-planning purposes to family trusts or entities controlled by Pellegrini (provided Pellegrini retains management control), shall not require the City's consent and shall not constitute a Change of Control, so long as the Pellegrini Interest is not reduced below the threshold set forth in Section 15.2.

### Section 15.1.3 Permitted Transfers for Approved Capital Raises.

Transfers of membership interests in Developer in connection with a capital raise (including for later Phases) that has been approved by both Lakeshore Capital Partners Fund III LP (pursuant to the Lakeshore Side Letter) and the City shall not require separate City consent for the assignment itself, provided the assignee expressly assumes Developer's obligations in writing. The City shall respond to any request for consent to a capital raise within thirty (30) days and shall not unreasonably withhold, condition, or delay such consent.

### Section 15.1.4 Assignment to Construction Lender or Foreclosure Purchaser.

Notwithstanding Section 15.1, Developer may assign this Agreement to the Construction Lender or to a Construction Lender-designee, or to a third-party purchaser at a foreclosure sale or pursuant to a deed-in-lieu of foreclosure, without the City's prior consent; or, if City consent is required, such consent shall not be unreasonably withheld, conditioned, or delayed, and the City shall respond within thirty (30) days of request. Any such assignee shall assume Developer's obligations under this Agreement in writing.

### Section 15.2 Change of Control.

A "Change of Control" shall be deemed to occur upon any of the following events: (a) the Pellegrini Interest falls below [50]% of the total membership interests in Developer; (b) Marcus J. Pellegrini ceases to serve as Manager and Chief Executive Officer of Developer and is not replaced by a person reasonably approved by the City (and, where applicable, by Lakeshore Capital Partners Fund III LP and the Construction Lender) within sixty (60) days following such cessation; (c) any single person or entity (other than the Pellegrini Family Trust, Lakeshore Capital Partners Fund III LP, or TransitWorks Equity Group LLC) acquires 25% or more of the membership interests in Developer; or (d) Developer merges with, consolidates into, or is acquired by another entity. Any Change of Control that occurs without the City's prior written consent shall constitute a material default under this Agreement, subject to the cure provisions of Section 13.2 and the 60-day replacement period in subsection (b).

### Section 15.3 Key Person Provision.

The parties acknowledge that the City's selection of Developer was based, in substantial part, on the qualifications, experience, and financial capacity of Marcus J. Pellegrini. Accordingly, Marcus J. Pellegrini shall serve as Manager and Chief Executive Officer of Developer and shall exercise day-to-day management authority over the Project. The Key Person requirement in Section 15.2(b) is intended as an alternative or supplement to rigid percentage-of-interest thresholds, providing the City with assurance of Pellegrini's active and material involvement while accommodating the internal transfers and approved capital raises contemplated by Sections 15.1.2 and 15.1.3.

[Drafting Note — Conflict C3 (assignment / change-of-control thresholds). Three different thresholds appear across the source documents: (i) the Term Sheet (Section 10) permits assignment without City consent only to an entity in which Pellegrini holds "at least a 50% controlling interest"; (ii) the Loan Term Sheet (Section 8.1) defines a Change of Control if Pellegrini ceases to hold "at least a [50]%" interest (bracketed, "subject to further discussion and negotiation"), or ceases as Manager/CEO, or a new investor acquires 25% or more; and (iii) the Lakeshore Side Letter (Section 5.2) defines a Change of Control if the Pellegrini Interest falls below 51%, or Pellegrini ceases as Manager/CEO (not replaced within 60 days by a person reasonably approved by Investor), or a new person acquires 25% or more, or a merger/consolidation/acquisition. The Lakeshore Side Letter (Section 4.4) expressly states that the 51% threshold is "intended to provide Investor with enhanced governance protections at a level above the assignment restriction threshold contemplated in the Term Sheet (which requires Pellegrini to hold at least fifty percent (50%) controlling interest)." Section 15.2 uses [50]% (bracketed) to track the Term Sheet and the Loan Term Sheet's bracketed figure, but the Lakeshore Side Letter's 51% threshold creates an internal Developer constraint: if the Pellegrini Interest falls below 51%, Lakeshore obtains enhanced consent rights over all amendments to this Agreement (not just Material Amendments) and a Change of Control remedy under the Side Letter. The three thresholds must be harmonized. The Lakeshore Side Letter (Section 8.3) commits Developer to negotiate Development Agreement assignment/change-of-control provisions that include (i) carve-outs for internal transfers among existing members, (ii) carve-outs for capital raises approved by both Investor and the City, (iii) a Key Person provision as an alternative/supplement to rigid percentage thresholds, and (iv) a reasonable cure period (not less than 60 days) before any assignment default. Sections 15.1.2, 15.1.3, 15.2(b), and 15.3 implement these commitments. Open items: (a) confirm whether the City will accept [50]% or insist on a different figure, and whether the Construction Lender's bracketed [50]% is finalized; (b) reconcile the 51% Lakeshore threshold with the Agreement's threshold (Developer may need to maintain the Pellegrini Interest at or above 51% to avoid triggering Lakeshore's enhanced rights, even if the Agreement threshold is 50%); (c) confirm the City accepts the Key Person alternative and the carve-outs. The Loan Term Sheet (Section 8.1) also provides that dilution of the Pellegrini Family Trust's interest below [50]%, "whether through additional equity raises, capital calls, anti-dilution adjustments, or admission of new members for Phases III and IV or otherwise, will require Lender's prior written consent and may trigger a Change of Control" — so later-phase capital raises implicate both the Construction Lender's and Lakeshore's consent rights.]

## ARTICLE 16. CONSTRUCTION LENDER PROTECTIONS

### Section 16.1 Lender Notice and Cure Rights.

The City shall provide the Construction Lender with prompt written notice of any default by Developer under this Agreement, and the Construction Lender shall have the right (but not the obligation) to cure such default. The Construction Lender's additional cure period shall be not less than thirty (30) days beyond Developer's cure period for monetary defaults and not less than sixty (60) days beyond Developer's cure period for non-monetary defaults, with such periods extended as necessary if the Construction Lender is diligently pursuing a cure that requires additional time (including time necessary to obtain possession of the Property through foreclosure or appointment of a receiver).

### Section 16.2 Lender Consent to Amendments.

No material amendment, modification, supplement, or termination of this Agreement shall be effective without the Construction Lender's prior written consent, which shall not be unreasonably withheld, conditioned, or delayed. "Material amendment" has the meaning set forth in the Lakeshore Side Letter (Section 2) and includes, without limitation, amendments that reduce or restructure any public incentive, accelerate or extend any phase deadline by more than six (6) months, increase Developer's financial obligations, modify affordable housing requirements, modify assignment or change-of-control restrictions, or alter any completion-assurance mechanism.

### Section 16.3 Subordination of City Interests.

Any lien, encumbrance, or security interest that the City may hold or acquire in the Property (other than the City's rights under the completion-assurance mechanism in Article 14, which are structured as contractual remedies rather than real-property interests) shall be subordinated to the Construction Lender's first-priority mortgage pursuant to a subordination and intercreditor agreement in form and substance acceptable to the Construction Lender.

### Section 16.4 Foreclosure; Assignment to Lender-Designee.

This Agreement shall be assignable to the Construction Lender or a Construction Lender-designee, or to a third-party purchaser at a foreclosure sale or pursuant to a deed-in-lieu of foreclosure, as provided in Section 15.1.4, without the City's prior consent (or, if consent is required, such consent shall not be unreasonably withheld, conditioned, or delayed, with a 30-day response). The Construction Lender's mortgage shall remain a first-priority lien on the Property and all improvements.

### Section 16.5 Estoppel Certificates.

The City shall deliver estoppel certificates to the Construction Lender upon reasonable request (not more than twice per calendar year), certifying the status of this Agreement, the existence or absence of any outstanding defaults by Developer, and such other customary matters as the Construction Lender may reasonably request.

### Section 16.6 Lender Review of Agreement.

This Agreement shall be delivered to the Construction Lender in draft form not less than fifteen (15) business days prior to its proposed execution date and shall not be executed until the Construction Lender has confirmed in writing that it is in form and substance acceptable to it.

[Drafting Note — Conflict C25 (Lender protections). This Article 16 implements the Construction Lender's required Development Agreement provisions set forth in Loan Term Sheet Section 12: (a) no reversionary interest (implemented in Article 14); (b) Lender notice and cure rights (Section 16.1); (c) Lender consent to material amendments (Section 16.2); (d) subordination of City interests (Section 16.3); (e) foreclosure/deed-in-lieu assignment (Sections 15.1.4, 16.4); and (f) estoppel certificates (Section 16.5). These provisions are necessary for the Construction Loan to close. The City may resist certain provisions — particularly Section 16.3 (subordination of City interests to the Construction Lender's mortgage) and Section 16.4 (assignment to a foreclosure purchaser without City consent) — as these limit the City's control over the Project following a Developer default. These are open negotiation items with the City. The Lakeshore Side Letter (Section 4.2) also requires Lakeshore's consent for Material Amendments, so amendments to this Agreement will require both Construction Lender and Lakeshore consent (Developer's external and internal constraints). Section 16.2 cross-references the Lakeshore Side Letter's definition of "Material Amendment" to promote consistency.]

[Drafting Note — Conflict C39 (cross-default DA↔Loan). The Loan Term Sheet (Section 9(5)) provides that a material default by Developer under this Agreement that is not cured within applicable cure periods, or any termination of this Agreement by the City for any reason, constitutes an Event of Default under the Construction Loan. The cure periods in Article 16.1 are intended to give the Construction Lender time to cure a Developer default before the loan defaults, but Developer should recognize that a City termination of this Agreement would cross-default the loan. Coordinate the cure periods and termination triggers accordingly.]

## ARTICLE 17. INDEMNIFICATION

### Section 17.1 Mutual Indemnification.

Each party shall indemnify, defend, and hold harmless the other party, and its officers, employees, agents, and representatives, from and against all claims, demands, actions, damages, losses, costs, liabilities, and expenses (including reasonable attorneys' fees and court costs) arising from or related to the indemnifying party's negligent acts or omissions, reckless conduct, or willful misconduct in connection with the performance of its obligations under this Agreement.

### Section 17.2 Developer Indemnification of City.

Developer shall indemnify, defend, and hold harmless the City from and against all claims, demands, actions, damages, losses, costs, liabilities, and expenses (including reasonable attorneys' fees) arising from or related to construction activities on the Property, including without limitation claims for personal injury, death, property damage, and workers' compensation, regardless of whether such claims are brought by Developer's employees, contractors, subcontractors, or third parties.

### Section 17.3 City Indemnification of Developer (Environmental).

The City shall indemnify, defend, and hold harmless Developer, its members, officers, employees, agents, contractors, and successors from and against all claims, demands, actions, losses, costs, damages, liabilities, and expenses (including reasonable attorneys' fees) arising from or related to pre-existing environmental conditions on the Property, as provided in Section 6.2.

### Section 17.4 Survival.

The indemnification obligations set forth in this Article 17, and the environmental indemnity in Section 6.2, shall survive the expiration or termination of this Agreement.

### Section 17.5 Prevailing Wage Indemnification.

Developer shall indemnify the City for any claims, penalties, or liabilities arising from prevailing wage violations on the Project to the extent attributable to Developer's or its contractors' non-compliance with the Prevailing Wage Act, subject to the scope of prevailing wage applicability set forth in Section 9.3.

[Drafting Note — The Term Sheet (Section 16) provides mutual indemnification, Developer indemnification of the City for construction activities, and City indemnification of Developer for pre-existing environmental conditions, with survival. Section 17.5 adds a prevailing wage indemnity as recommended by the TIF Memo (Section V.C), scoped to the prevailing wage applicability in Section 9.3. Developer should seek to limit the prevailing wage indemnity to violations on components where prevailing wage is statutorily required or contractually agreed, rather than all Project components.]

## ARTICLE 18. DISPUTE RESOLUTION

### Section 18.1 Negotiation.

The parties shall first attempt to resolve any dispute arising under or related to this Agreement through good-faith negotiation between the City Manager (or designee) and Developer's CEO (or designee) for a period of thirty (30) days following written notice of the dispute by either party.

### Section 18.2 Mediation.

If the dispute is not resolved through negotiation within the 30-day period, the dispute shall be submitted to non-binding mediation before the Lakemont Mediation Center, or such other neutral mediator as the parties may agree. The costs of mediation shall be shared equally by the parties, and each party shall bear its own attorneys' fees.

### Section 18.3 Binding Arbitration.

If the dispute is not resolved through mediation within sixty (60) days following submission to the mediator, the dispute shall be submitted to final and binding arbitration administered under the Commercial Arbitration Rules of the American Arbitration Association, conducted by a single arbitrator with experience in real estate development matters. All mediation and arbitration proceedings shall be conducted in Cook County or Lake County, Illinois.

### Section 18.4 Provisional Remedies.

Nothing in this Article 18 shall prevent either party from seeking temporary restraining orders, preliminary injunctions, or other provisional judicial relief where necessary to prevent irreparable harm, pending resolution of the dispute through the procedures set forth above.

### Section 18.5 Governing Law.

This Agreement shall be governed by and construed in accordance with the laws of the State of Illinois, without regard to conflict-of-laws principles.

[Drafting Note — Conflict C22 (dispute resolution). The Term Sheet (Section 17) provides a three-step process: negotiation (30 days), mediation (Lakemont Mediation Center, 60 days), and binding AAA arbitration (single arbitrator, Cook or Lake County). The Lakeshore Side Letter (Section 10.2) uses a similar negotiation/mediation/arbitration structure under AAA rules in Cook County. The Loan Term Sheet (Section 13), by contrast, provides for litigation in Cook County state or federal courts (not arbitration). The dispute-resolution mechanisms in the different agreements can coexist because they govern different contracts, but Developer should be aware that a dispute could simultaneously proceed under this Agreement's arbitration framework and the Construction Loan's litigation framework if the same facts implicate both contracts. The Term Sheet's arbitration framework is adopted here. Open item: confirm the City accepts AAA arbitration (some municipalities resist binding arbitration of public contracts); confirm venue county (Cook or Lake) consistent with the county determination in the drafting note to Section 1.1.1.]

Any use of TIF revenues in connection with the Project shall comply in all respects with the TIF Act and the Lakemont Central TIF District Redevelopment Plan and Project as adopted by Ordinance No. 2019-42, as amended. Name: Marcus J. Pellegrini
Title: Manager and Chief Executive Officer
Date: _____________________________

**CITY OF LAKEMONT, ILLINOIS**

By: _______________________________
Name: Gerald T. Fong

APPROVED AS TO FORM:

___________________________________
Rebecca A. Sinclair, Esq.
Corporation Counsel, City of Lakemont, Illinois
Date: _____________________________

___________________________________
Jonathan Ashford
Ashford, Keene & Mulvaney LLP
Outside Municipal Counsel

---

- C26 (Sections 16.2, 21.3): Lakeshore consent rights over DA — Lakeshore Side Letter §4.
- C27 (Sections 5.2, 6.3, 9.3): Minimum IRR 14% — Lakeshore Side Letter §3 constrains Developer negotiation.

**Open items (unresolved terms):**
