## ARTICLE 14. COMPLETION ASSURANCE (ALTERNATIVE TO REVERSIONARY INTEREST)

### Section 14.1 No Reversionary Interest.

The parties acknowledge that the Construction Lender has advised that any reversionary interest, right of re-entry, possibility of reverter, or other defeasible fee estate in favor of the City would render Developer's fee simple title uninsurable, subordinate the Construction Lender's first-priority mortgage, prevent foreclosure, and render the Construction Loan unsecurable. Accordingly, notwithstanding Resolution Condition #8(h), the Property shall not be subject to any reversionary interest, right of re-entry, possibility of reverter, fee simple determinable, fee simple subject to condition subsequent, or any other form of defeasible fee estate in favor of the City or any other party. Developer's fee simple title to the Property shall remain absolute and indefeasible, subject only to Permitted Exceptions.

### Section 14.2 Alternative Completion-Assurance Mechanism.

In lieu of the reversionary interest contemplated by Resolution Condition #8(h), the parties adopt the following alternative mechanism to satisfy the City's policy objective of ensuring timely Phase I completion while preserving Developer's fee simple absolute title and the Construction Lender's first-priority security interest:

(a) **Phase I Completion Deadline.** Developer shall achieve Substantial Completion of Phase I by September 30, 2028, as such deadline may be extended by Force Majeure under Section 12.3 and by the Construction Lender-approved extension periods.

(b) **Cure Periods.** If Developer fails to achieve Phase I Substantial Completion by the deadline (as extended), the City shall provide written notice of such failure. Developer shall have the cure periods set forth in Section 13.2 (90 days, plus additional time if diligently pursuing cure), followed by the Construction Lender's additional cure rights under Article 16 (not less than 60 days beyond Developer's cure period for non-monetary defaults, with extension if the Construction Lender is diligently pursuing a cure, including time necessary to obtain possession through foreclosure or a receiver).

(c) **Contractual Repurchase Option.** If, after expiration of all cure periods (including the Construction Lender's extended cure period), Developer has failed to achieve Phase I Substantial Completion and the Construction Lender has not cured or completed Phase I, the City shall have a contractual option to repurchase the Property, exercisable only upon (i) Developer's failure to achieve Phase I Substantial Completion within the agreed timeframe, (ii) expiration of all cure periods (including the Construction Lender's extended cure period), and (iii) the Construction Lender's prior written consent or full satisfaction of the outstanding Construction Loan balance. The repurchase price shall be the lesser of (A) the original purchase price paid by Developer ($9,200,000) or (B) the then-current fair market value of the Property as determined by an independent MAI appraiser mutually selected by the parties. The repurchase option shall be subordinate to the Construction Lender's mortgage and shall not be exercisable until the Construction Lender's mortgage is satisfied or the Construction Lender consents.

(d) **Enhanced Performance Security.** The $15,000,000 Phase I performance security under Article 8 shall be available to the City as liquidated security for Developer's Phase I completion obligation, in addition to the repurchase option. The performance security shall be the City's primary financial remedy for a Phase I completion failure, to be drawn upon only after expiration of all cure periods.

(e) **No Automatic Reversion.** Title to the Property shall not automatically revert to the City under any circumstance. The City's remedies for a Phase I completion failure are limited to the contractual remedies set forth in this Article 14 (the repurchase option and the performance security) and the termination and damage remedies under Article 13, in each case subject to the Construction Lender's rights under Article 16.

[Drafting Note — Conflict C1 (reversionary interest — central conflict). This Article 14 is the most critical provision in this draft and the single most important open item. Resolution Condition #8(h) requires Developer to grant the City a reversionary interest with automatic reversion of fee simple title if Phase I is not Substantially Complete within 36 months of conveyance. The Construction Lender (Loan Term Sheet Sections 4.2, 7.1(1), 12(a), and the "Specific Concern" discussion in Section 12) states unequivocally that it "cannot commit to a loan secured by a mortgage on property that is subject to a reversionary interest or any form of defeasible fee estate" and that incorporation of the reversionary interest "as stated in the Resolution, would make the Loan impossible to close." The Construction Lender suggests five alternative mechanisms: (i) a contractual repurchase option exercisable only after cure periods and Construction Lender consent/satisfaction; (ii) a performance escrow/additional letter of credit; (iii) liquidated damages; (iv) an enhanced completion guaranty; and (v) a right of first refusal subordinate to the mortgage. Section 14.2 adopts a combination of alternatives (i) and (ii) (repurchase option plus the existing $15,000,000 performance security), which the Construction Lender specifically identified as acceptable. The Lakeshore Side Letter (Section 7(d)) requires Investor consent to any clawback, reversionary interest, right of re-entry, repurchase option, or similar mechanism, and acknowledges that the parties "must negotiate terms compatible with the Construction Lender's requirements and Investor's interests." Accordingly, this Article 14 requires both City agreement (to deviate from the literal terms of Resolution Condition #8(h), likely via a further City Council action or a Corporation Counsel determination that the alternative satisfies the condition) and Lakeshore consent. This must be resolved before execution; if the City insists on the literal reversionary interest, the Construction Loan cannot close and the Project financing fails.]

[Drafting Note — Conflict C2 (36-month clock). Resolution Condition #8(h) measures the 36-month period from the date of conveyance. Section 14.2(a) instead measures the completion obligation against the Phase I Substantial Completion deadline of September 30, 2028 (as extended by Force Majeure and Construction Lender extensions), which is tied to the March 1, 2026 groundbreaking. This avoids the timing mismatch under which a 36-month-from-conveyance clock could expire before the Substantial Completion deadline. The City should confirm acceptance of measuring the completion assurance against the Substantial Completion deadline rather than a fixed 36-month-from-conveyance period.]

## ARTICLE 15. ASSIGNMENT AND CHANGE OF CONTROL

### Section 15.1 Restriction on Assignment.

Developer may not assign this Agreement, or any interest therein, in whole or in part, without the prior written consent of the City, which consent may be withheld in the City's reasonable discretion; provided, that the City's consent shall not be required, and shall not be unreasonably withheld, conditioned, or delayed, for the assignments described in Sections 15.1.1, 15.1.2, and 15.1.3. Any purported assignment in violation of this Article 15 shall be void and of no force and effect and shall constitute a material default. For purposes of this Article 15, "assignment" includes any direct or indirect transfer, conveyance, pledge, hypothecation, or other disposition of this Agreement or any interest therein, whether voluntary or involuntary, by operation of law or otherwise.

### Section 15.1.1 Permitted Assignment to Pellegrini-Controlled Entity.

Developer may assign this Agreement without the City's prior written consent to an entity in which Marcus J. Pellegrini (or the Pellegrini Family Trust) holds at least a 50% controlling interest and which entity expressly assumes in writing all of Developer's obligations under this Agreement. Developer shall provide the City with written notice of any such permitted assignment, together with a copy of the assignment and assumption agreement, within fifteen (15) days of the effective date.

### Section 15.1.2 Permitted Transfers Among Existing Members.

Transfers of membership interests in Developer among the existing members (Pellegrini Family Trust, Lakeshore Capital Partners Fund III LP, and TransitWorks Equity Group LLC), and transfers by Marcus J. Pellegrini for estate-planning purposes to family trusts or entities controlled by Pellegrini (provided Pellegrini retains management control), shall not require the City's consent and shall not constitute a Change of Control, so long as the Pellegrini Interest is not reduced below the threshold set forth in Section 15.2.

### Section 15.1.3 Permitted Transfers for Approved Capital Raises.

Transfers of membership interests in Developer in connection with a capital raise (including for later Phases) that has been approved by both Lakeshore Capital Partners Fund III LP (pursuant to the Lakeshore Side Letter) and the City shall not require separate City consent for the assignment itself, provided the assignee expressly assumes Developer's obligations in writing. The City shall respond to any request for consent to a capital raise within thirty (30) days and shall not unreasonably withhold, condition, or delay such consent.

### Section 15.1.4 Assignment to Construction Lender or Foreclosure Purchaser.

Notwithstanding Section 15.1, Developer may assign this Agreement to the Construction Lender or to a Construction Lender-designee, or to a third-party purchaser at a foreclosure sale or pursuant to a deed-in-lieu of foreclosure, without the City's prior consent; or, if City consent is required, such consent shall not be unreasonably withheld, conditioned, or delayed, and the City shall respond within thirty (30) days of request. Any such assignee shall assume Developer's obligations under this Agreement in writing.

### Section 15.2 Change of Control.

A "Change of Control" shall be deemed to occur upon any of the following events: (a) the Pellegrini Interest falls below [50]% of the total membership interests in Developer; (b) Marcus J. Pellegrini ceases to serve as Manager and Chief Executive Officer of Developer and is not replaced by a person reasonably approved by the City (and, where applicable, by Lakeshore Capital Partners Fund III LP and the Construction Lender) within sixty (60) days following such cessation; (c) any single person or entity (other than the Pellegrini Family Trust, Lakeshore Capital Partners Fund III LP, or TransitWorks Equity Group LLC) acquires 25% or more of the membership interests in Developer; or (d) Developer merges with, consolidates into, or is acquired by another entity. Any Change of Control that occurs without the City's prior written consent shall constitute a material default under this Agreement, subject to the cure provisions of Section 13.2 and the 60-day replacement period in subsection (b).

### Section 15.3 Key Person Provision.

The parties acknowledge that the City's selection of Developer was based, in substantial part, on the qualifications, experience, and financial capacity of Marcus J. Pellegrini. Accordingly, Marcus J. Pellegrini shall serve as Manager and Chief Executive Officer of Developer and shall exercise day-to-day management authority over the Project. The Key Person requirement in Section 15.2(b) is intended as an alternative or supplement to rigid percentage-of-interest thresholds, providing the City with assurance of Pellegrini's active and material involvement while accommodating the internal transfers and approved capital raises contemplated by Sections 15.1.2 and 15.1.3.

[Drafting Note — Conflict C3 (assignment / change-of-control thresholds). ## ARTICLE 16. CONSTRUCTION LENDER PROTECTIONS

### Section 16.1 Lender Notice and Cure Rights.

The City shall provide the Construction Lender with prompt written notice of any default by Developer under this Agreement, and the Construction Lender shall have the right (but not the obligation) to cure such default. The Construction Lender's additional cure period shall be not less than thirty (30) days beyond Developer's cure period for monetary defaults and not less than sixty (60) days beyond Developer's cure period for non-monetary defaults, with such periods extended as necessary if the Construction Lender is diligently pursuing a cure that requires additional time (including time necessary to obtain possession of the Property through foreclosure or appointment of a receiver).

### Section 16.2 Lender Consent to Amendments.

No material amendment, modification, supplement, or termination of this Agreement shall be effective without the Construction Lender's prior written consent, which shall not be unreasonably withheld, conditioned, or delayed. "Material amendment" has the meaning set forth in the Lakeshore Side Letter (Section 2) and includes, without limitation, amendments that reduce or restructure any public incentive, accelerate or extend any phase deadline by more than six (6) months, increase Developer's financial obligations, modify affordable housing requirements, modify assignment or change-of-control restrictions, or alter any completion-assurance mechanism.

### Section 16.3 Subordination of City Interests.

Any lien, encumbrance, or security interest that the City may hold or acquire in the Property (other than the City's rights under the completion-assurance mechanism in Article 14, which are structured as contractual remedies rather than real-property interests) shall be subordinated to the Construction Lender's first-priority mortgage pursuant to a subordination and intercreditor agreement in form and substance acceptable to the Construction Lender.

### Section 16.4 Foreclosure; Assignment to Lender-Designee.

This Agreement shall be assignable to the Construction Lender or a Construction Lender-designee, or to a third-party purchaser at a foreclosure sale or pursuant to a deed-in-lieu of foreclosure, as provided in Section 15.1.4, without the City's prior consent (or, if consent is required, such consent shall not be unreasonably withheld, conditioned, or delayed, with a 30-day response). The Construction Lender's mortgage shall remain a first-priority lien on the Property and all improvements.

### Section 16.5 Estoppel Certificates.

The City shall deliver estoppel certificates to the Construction Lender upon reasonable request (not more than twice per calendar year), certifying the status of this Agreement, the existence or absence of any outstanding defaults by Developer, and such other customary matters as the Construction Lender may reasonably request.

### Section 16.6 Lender Review of Agreement.

This Agreement shall be delivered to the Construction Lender in draft form not less than fifteen (15) business days prior to its proposed execution date and shall not be executed until the Construction Lender has confirmed in writing that it is in form and substance acceptable to it.

[Drafting Note — Conflict C25 (Lender protections). This Article 16 implements the Construction Lender's required Development Agreement provisions set forth in Loan Term Sheet Section 12: (a) no reversionary interest (implemented in Article 14); (b) Lender notice and cure rights (Section 16.1); (c) Lender consent to material amendments (Section 16.2); (d) subordination of City interests (Section 16.3); (e) foreclosure/deed-in-lieu assignment (Sections 15.1.4, 16.4); and (f) estoppel certificates (Section 16.5). These provisions are necessary for the Construction Loan to close. The City may resist certain provisions — particularly Section 16.3 (subordination of City interests to the Construction Lender's mortgage) and Section 16.4 (assignment to a foreclosure purchaser without City consent) — as these limit the City's control over the Project following a Developer default. These are open negotiation items with the City. The Lakeshore Side Letter (Section 4.2) also requires Lakeshore's consent for Material Amendments, so amendments to this Agreement will require both Construction Lender and Lakeshore consent (Developer's external and internal constraints). Section 16.2 cross-references the Lakeshore Side Letter's definition of "Material Amendment" to promote consistency.]

[Drafting Note — Conflict C39 (cross-default DA↔Loan). The Loan Term Sheet (Section 9(5)) provides that a material default by Developer under this Agreement that is not cured within applicable cure periods, or any termination of this Agreement by the City for any reason, constitutes an Event of Default under the Construction Loan. The cure periods in Article 16.1 are intended to give the Construction Lender time to cure a Developer default before the loan defaults, but Developer should recognize that a City termination of this Agreement would cross-default the loan. Coordinate the cure periods and termination triggers accordingly.]

## ARTICLE 17. INDEMNIFICATION

### Section 17.1 Mutual Indemnification.

Each party shall indemnify, defend, and hold harmless the other party, and its officers, employees, agents, and representatives, from and against all claims, demands, actions, damages, losses, costs, liabilities, and expenses (including reasonable attorneys' fees and court costs) arising from or related to the indemnifying party's negligent acts or omissions, reckless conduct, or willful misconduct in connection with the performance of its obligations under this Agreement.

### Section 17.2 Developer Indemnification of City.

Developer shall indemnify, defend, and hold harmless the City from and against all claims, demands, actions, damages, losses, costs, liabilities, and expenses (including reasonable attorneys' fees) arising from or related to construction activities on the Property, including without limitation claims for personal injury, death, property damage, and workers' compensation, regardless of whether such claims are brought by Developer's employees, contractors, subcontractors, or third parties.

### Section 17.3 City Indemnification of Developer (Environmental).

The City shall indemnify, defend, and hold harmless Developer, its members, officers, employees, agents, contractors, and successors from and against all claims, demands, actions, losses, costs, damages, liabilities, and expenses (including reasonable attorneys' fees) arising from or related to pre-existing environmental conditions on the Property, as provided in Section 6.2.

### Section 17.4 Survival.

The indemnification obligations set forth in this Article 17, and the environmental indemnity in Section 6.2, shall survive the expiration or termination of this Agreement.

### Section 17.5 Prevailing Wage Indemnification.

Developer shall indemnify the City for any claims, penalties, or liabilities arising from prevailing wage violations on the Project to the extent attributable to Developer's or its contractors' non-compliance with the Prevailing Wage Act, subject to the scope of prevailing wage applicability set forth in Section 9.3.

[Drafting Note — The Term Sheet (Section 16) provides mutual indemnification, Developer indemnification of the City for construction activities, and City indemnification of Developer for pre-existing environmental conditions, with survival. Section 17.5 adds a prevailing wage indemnity as recommended by the TIF Memo (Section V.C), scoped to the prevailing wage applicability in Section 9.3. Developer should seek to limit the prevailing wage indemnity to violations on components where prevailing wage is statutorily required or contractually agreed, rather than all Project components.]

---

## ARTICLE 18. DISPUTE RESOLUTION

### Section 18.1 Negotiation.

The parties shall first attempt to resolve any dispute arising under or related to this Agreement through good-faith negotiation between the City Manager (or designee) and Developer's CEO (or designee) for a period of thirty (30) days following written notice of the dispute by either party.

### Section 18.2 Mediation.

If the dispute is not resolved through negotiation within the 30-day period, the dispute shall be submitted to non-binding mediation before the Lakemont Mediation Center, or such other neutral mediator as the parties may agree. The costs of mediation shall be shared equally by the parties, and each party shall bear its own attorneys' fees.

### Section 18.3 Binding Arbitration.

If the dispute is not resolved through mediation within sixty (60) days following submission to the mediator, the dispute shall be submitted to final and binding arbitration administered under the Commercial Arbitration Rules of the American Arbitration Association, conducted by a single arbitrator with experience in real estate development matters. All mediation and arbitration proceedings shall be conducted in Cook County or Lake County, Illinois.

### Section 18.4 Provisional Remedies.

Nothing in this Article 18 shall prevent either party from seeking temporary restraining orders, preliminary injunctions, or other provisional judicial relief where necessary to prevent irreparable harm, pending resolution of the dispute through the procedures set forth above.

### Section 18.5 Governing Law.

This Agreement shall be governed by and construed in accordance with the laws of the State of Illinois, without regard to conflict-of-laws principles.

[Drafting Note — Conflict C22 (dispute resolution). The Term Sheet (Section 17) provides a three-step process: negotiation (30 days), mediation (Lakemont Mediation Center, 60 days), and binding AAA arbitration (single arbitrator, Cook or Lake County). The Lakeshore Side Letter (Section 10.2) uses a similar negotiation/mediation/arbitration structure under AAA rules in Cook County. The Loan Term Sheet (Section 13), by contrast, provides for litigation in Cook County state or federal courts (not arbitration). The dispute-resolution mechanisms in the different agreements can coexist because they govern different contracts, but Developer should be aware that a dispute could simultaneously proceed under this Agreement's arbitration framework and the Construction Loan's litigation framework if the same facts implicate both contracts. The Term Sheet's arbitration framework is adopted here. Open item: confirm the City accepts AAA arbitration (some municipalities resist binding arbitration of public contracts); confirm venue county (Cook or Lake) consistent with the county determination in the drafting note to Section 1.1.1.]

## ARTICLE 19. REPRESENTATIONS AND WARRANTIES

### Section 19.1 Developer Representations.

Developer represents and warrants that: (a) it is a limited liability company duly organized, validly existing, and in good standing under the laws of the State of Illinois; (b) it has full power and authority to enter into this Agreement and to perform its obligations hereunder, and the execution and delivery of this Agreement has been duly authorized; (c) the execution and delivery of this Agreement will not conflict with or violate Developer's organizational documents, the Construction Loan Documents, or any other material agreement to which Developer is a party; (d) the ownership structure of Developer is as set forth in Section 1.1.4; (e) Developer has the financial capacity, development experience, and organizational resources necessary to complete the Project; (f) Developer's entity-level net worth is approximately $185,000,000 and its bonding capacity is up to $75,000,000; and (g) there are no environmental conditions on the Property other than those disclosed in the Phase II ESA.

### Section 19.2 City Representations.

The City represents and warrants that: (a) it has full power and authority to enter into this Agreement and to perform its obligations hereunder, and the execution and delivery of this Agreement has been duly authorized, including by the City Council; (b) the City has good and marketable fee simple title to the Property, free and clear of all liens, encumbrances, and adverse claims (except Permitted Exceptions), and has the legal authority to convey the Property to Developer; (c) the Property is located within the TIF District and TIF funds are legally available for the purposes described in this Agreement, subject to annual appropriation and the availability of increment; (d) the rezoning and parcel consolidation will be completed as provided in Article 2; and (e) the City has disclosed all known environmental conditions on the Property through the Phase II ESA and other materials provided to Developer.

[Drafting Note — The Term Sheet (Section 18.1) contemplates customary representations and warranties, including the City's representation of good and marketable fee simple title and the availability of TIF funds subject to annual appropriation and increment availability. Section 19.2(e) adds a City representation regarding disclosure of known environmental conditions, supporting the City's indemnity for pre-existing environmental conditions in Sections 6.2 and 17.3. The Phase II ESA (Section 4.2.4) notes that the City acquired Parcels 016 and 017 in 2018 "with knowledge of the environmental conditions" and that the City's 2018 purchase records reference a prior Phase I ESA identifying the UST farm and metal finishing operations as potential contamination sources.]

## ARTICLE 20. REPORTING AND ACCESS

### Section 20.1 Reports to the City.

Developer shall provide the City with: (a) quarterly construction progress reports within thirty (30) days following the end of each calendar quarter during active construction; (b) quarterly local hiring reports within thirty (30) days following the end of each calendar quarter; and (c) annual income certification documentation for Affordable Units throughout the affordability restriction period.

### Section 20.2 Reports to Equity Investors and Lender.

Developer shall provide Lakeshore Capital Partners Fund III LP and the Construction Lender with the reports required under the Lakeshore Side Letter and the Construction Loan Documents, respectively, including monthly construction progress reports, quarterly and annual financial statements, copies of material correspondence with the City and IEPA, and prompt notice of any default or alleged default under this Agreement.

### Section 20.3 Access.

The City shall have reasonable access to the Property during normal business hours upon not fewer than three (3) business days' advance written notice for inspection and monitoring purposes, subject to safety and security requirements.

[Drafting Note — Conflict C38 (reporting overlap). The Term Sheet, the Lakeshore Side Letter (Section 6), and the Loan Term Sheet (Sections 6.1, 6.2) each impose overlapping reporting obligations with different frequencies and recipients: the Term Sheet requires quarterly construction and local-hiring reports to the City; the Lakeshore Side Letter requires monthly construction reports (within 15 days of month-end), quarterly unaudited financials (within 45 days), annual audited financials (within 90 days), copies of all material City/Construction Lender/IEPA correspondence, prompt notice (within 5 business days) of any default, and copies of all TIF reimbursement requests; and the Loan Term Sheet requires monthly draw requests, annual audited financials (within 120 days), and quarterly unaudited financials (within 45 days). Sections 20.1 and 20.2 allocate reports by recipient. Developer should seek to harmonize the financial-statement deadlines (the Lakeshore Side Letter's 90-day annual deadline is shorter than the Loan Term Sheet's 120-day deadline) and to avoid duplicative reporting burdens where possible. This is an open item.]

## ARTICLE 21. MISCELLANEOUS

### Section 21.1 Notices.

All notices shall be in writing and deemed delivered upon personal delivery, upon delivery by nationally recognized overnight courier, or three (3) business days following deposit in the U.S. mail, registered or certified, return receipt requested, postage prepaid, addressed as follows:

**If to Developer:** Granite City Development LLC, 900 West Monroe Street, Suite 2200, Chicago, IL 60607, Attn: Marcus J. Pellegrini, Manager/CEO; with a copy to Thornfield & Pratt LLP, 311 South Wacker Drive, Suite 5400, Chicago, IL 60606, Attn: Catherine M. Albrecht.

**If to City:** City of Lakemont, Office of the City Manager, 200 Civic Center Drive, Lakemont, IL 60048, Attn: Gerald T. Fong, City Manager; with a copy to Office of Corporation Counsel, City of Lakemont, 200 Civic Center Drive, Lakemont, IL 60048, Attn: Rebecca A. Sinclair, Esq.; and with a copy to Ashford, Keene & Mulvaney LLP, 150 North Michigan Avenue, Suite 3200, Chicago, IL 60601, Attn: Jonathan Ashford.

Either party may change its notice address by written notice to the other party.

### Section 21.2 Confidentiality; FOIA.

The terms and contents of this Agreement are confidential and shall not be disclosed by either party to any third party, except: (a) to each party's legal counsel, financial advisors, accountants, lenders, and equity investors, as reasonably necessary; (b) as required by applicable law or governmental regulation, including the Illinois Freedom of Information Act, 5 ILCS 140/, as applicable to the City; and (c) to the extent necessary for City Council proceedings, committee meetings, and other governmental approvals. The parties acknowledge that, as a public contract, this Agreement may be subject to disclosure under the Illinois Freedom of Information Act.

### Section 21.3 Amendments.

This Agreement may be amended, modified, or supplemented only by a written instrument executed by both parties, subject to the Construction Lender's consent rights under Section 16.2 and Lakeshore Capital Partners Fund III LP's consent rights under the Lakeshore Side Letter.

### Section 21.4 Severability.

If any provision of this Agreement is held invalid or unenforceable, such invalidity shall not affect other provisions that can be given effect without the invalid provision, and the provisions are severable.

### Section 21.5 Entire Agreement; Supersession.

This Agreement, together with its exhibits and the documents referenced herein, constitutes the entire agreement between the parties with respect to the subject matter hereof and supersedes the Term Sheet in its entirety upon execution. In the event of any conflict between this Agreement and the Term Sheet, this Agreement controls.

### Section 21.6 Counterparts; Electronic Signatures.

This Agreement may be executed in counterparts, each deemed an original, and signatures transmitted by electronic means shall be deemed original signatures.

### Section 21.7 No Third-Party Beneficiaries.

Except for the Construction Lender's rights under Article 16, this Agreement is for the sole benefit of the parties and does not confer rights on any other person.

### Section 21.8 Expenses.

Each party shall bear its own costs and expenses incurred in connection with the negotiation and preparation of this Agreement, including legal fees, consultant fees, and other professional expenses.

### Section 21.9 Compliance with Laws.

Each party shall comply with all applicable laws, codes, and ordinances in the performance of its obligations under this Agreement, including the TIF Act, the Prevailing Wage Act, and the Illinois Environmental Protection Act.

### Section 21.10 TIF Compliance.

Any use of TIF revenues in connection with the Project shall comply in all respects with the TIF Act and the Lakemont Central TIF District Redevelopment Plan and Project as adopted by Ordinance No. 2019-42, as amended. All TIF-eligible redevelopment project costs shall be determined and reimbursed in accordance with applicable Illinois law and this Agreement.

{"content": "# Response Summary

## Deliverable
**`development-agreement-draft.docx`** — a developer-side draft of the Development Agreement for the Lakemont Station Commons mixed-use transit-oriented development, between Granite City Development LLC (Developer) and the City of Lakemont, Illinois.

A companion markdown source (`development-agreement-draft.md`) is also included.

## Source documents reviewed
All seven documents in the workspace were read in full before drafting:
1. City Council Resolution No. R-2025-017 (conditional approval + 8 conditions)
2. Negotiated Term Sheet (Developer/City, Jan. 22, 2025) — principal business terms
3. First Prairie National Bank preliminary construction loan term sheet ($210M)
4. Lakeshore Capital Partners Fund III LP side letter (25% member consent/anti-dilution/14% IRR)
5. Phase II Environmental Site Assessment executive summary (Greenfield, Nov. 8, 2024)
6. Developer RFP response narrative (Dec. 16, 2024)
7. Corporation Counsel TIF district summary memorandum (Mar. 5, 2025)

## Approach
- Drafted from the **Developer's perspective** throughout (favorable cure periods, force majeure, termination rights; City bears pre-existing environmental liability and cost overruns; TIF priority/extension covenant; land write-down and City infrastructure not charged against the TIF cap; alternative completion-assurance mechanism preserving fee simple title; Lender protections necessary for financing).
- Embedded **46 bracketed drafting notes** (`[Drafting Note — …]`) flagging cross-document conflicts and open items, each citing the specific source document and section.
- Organized as a complete 22-article agreement with recitals, definitions, conditions precedent, conveyance, incentives, environmental, financing, performance security, development obligations, community benefits, affordable housing, phasing/force majeure, default/cure/termination, completion assurance, assignment/change-of-control, lender protections, indemnification, dispute resolution, representations, reporting, miscellaneous, and exhibits.
- Concluded with a **consolidated index** of all 40 cross-document conflicts (C1–C40) and 15 open items (O1–O15).

## Key cross-document conflicts flagged
- **C1 (central):** Resolution Condition #8(h) requires a reversionary interest; the Loan Term Sheet (§§4.2, 12) prohibits any defeasible fee and states it would make the loan impossible to close; the Lakeshore Side Letter (§7(d)) requires consent for any clawback. Article 14 substitutes an alternative mechanism (contractual repurchase option + enhanced performance security) and flags this as the single most important open item requiring City agreement and Lakeshore consent.
- **C3:** Three different change-of-control thresholds (50% Term Sheet / [50]% Loan Term Sheet / 51% Lakeshore Side Letter), harmonized via a Key Person provision plus carve-outs and a 60-day cure.
- **C10/C11/C12:** TIF shortfall risk, land write-down vs. TIF cap, and City infrastructure funding source (per the TIF Memo).
- **C13/C18/C19:** Prevailing wage scope, the new living-wage obligation, and local-hiring goal vs. requirement.
- **C15/C16:** NFR vs. RAP as condition precedent, phased conveyance, and the equity-demonstration deadline.
- Plus parcel acquisition history, acreages, RFP number, county jurisdiction, affordable-unit count, CGL aggregate, force majeure cap, dispute resolution, condemnation threshold, and lender-protection negotiation items.

## Validation
The .docx passed `validate.py` (ZIP integrity, XML well-formedness, content-type registration, relationship consistency — exit 0). Tables (Sources & Uses; Phasing Schedule) and all heading/section structure rendered correctly.", "file_path": "/workspace/output/response.md"}

text:
The deliverable is complete and validated. Here is a summary of what I produced.

## Deliverable: `development-agreement-draft.docx`

I read all seven source documents in full before drafting, then produced a **developer-side Development Agreement** for the Lakemont Station Commons project (Granite City Development LLC vs. City of Lakemont), with **46 bracketed drafting notes** flagging cross-document conflicts and open items.

### Structure
A complete 22-article agreement: Recitals/Definitions → Conditions Precedent → The Project (4 phases) → Land Conveyance → Public Incentives → Environmental Matters → Project Financing → Performance Security → Development Obligations → Community Benefits → Affordable Housing → Phasing/Force Majeure → Default/Cure/Termination → **Completion Assurance (alternative to reversionary interest)** → Assignment/Change of Control → **Construction Lender Protections** → Indemnification → Dispute Resolution → Representations → Reporting → Miscellaneous → Exhibits. It closes with a consolidated index of 40 conflicts (C1–C40) and 15 open items (O1–O15).

### Developer's posture reflected throughout
- City bears pre-existing environmental liability, cost overruns, vapor mitigation, and off-site investigation costs (or first-priority TIF reimbursement)
- TIF reimbursement priority (remediation/infrastructure first), good-faith extension covenant, interest on delayed reimbursements, and shortfall not a default
- Land write-down and City infrastructure **not** charged against the $48M TIF cap
- Favorable cure periods, 18-month force majeure, strong Developer termination rights
- Lender protections (notice/cure, consent, subordination, foreclosure assignment, estoppel) necessary to close the $210M loan

### Most important flagged conflict (C1)
Resolution Condition #8(h) requires a **reversionary interest**, but the Loan Term Sheet (§§4.2, 12) states any defeasible fee would make the loan **impossible to close**, and the Lakeshore Side Letter (§7(d)) requires consent for any clawback. Article 14 substitutes an alternative (contractual repurchase option + enhanced performance security) and flags this as the single most critical open item requiring City agreement and Lakeshore consent before execution.

Other notable conflicts flagged: the three competing change-of-control thresholds (50%/[50]%/51%), TIF shortfall risk, prevailing-wage scope, the new living-wage obligation, NFR-vs-RAP as a condition precedent, phased conveyance, the equity-demonstration deadline, and several factual discrepancies (parcel acquisition history, acreages, RFP number, county jurisdiction).

The .docx passed schema/ZIP/XML validation (exit 0), with tables and heading structure rendering correctly. A markdown source and a response summary are also in the output directory.

Before you finish, run a mandatory COMPLETENESS AUDIT of your deliverable(s) against the source record. Do not assume your first draft covered everything — the most common failure on this kind of task is a deliverable that is polished but omits distinct, individually-graded points the record supports.

Do this now, as real tool calls (not in your head):
1. RE-LIST every distinct issue/requirement the source documents raise. Re-read the key source files and enumerate each separate problem, gap, discrepancy, nonconformance, or required element — one per item, not bundled. Include the ones you already wrote about AND any you have not yet addressed.
2. CROSS-CHECK your current deliverable against that list. For each item, is it actually present and explicitly addressed in the output file? Open the output file and verify by content, not memory. List every item that is missing, vague, bundled into another point, or only implied.
3. CHECK EACH REQUIRED OUTPUT / DIMENSION: confirm every deliverable the task asked for exists, and that any required comparison, matrix, or dimension (e.g. every jurisdiction, every party, every provision, every document in the file) is covered — do not drop a category because it seemed minor.
4. FILL THE GAPS: use edit/write to add every missing item with its own explicit treatment, drawing the specific facts from the source documents (cite the source). Do not fabricate issues the record does not support; do add the ones it does that you skipped.
5. Re-validate any binary deliverable per its skill manual, then finish.

If a thorough re-read finds nothing missing, say so briefly and finish. Otherwise, make the additions now.
I'll run a real completeness audit. Let me re-read the key source files to enumerate every distinct issue, then cross-check against the actual output file content.

result:
**[RESOLUTION NO. R-2025-017]{.underline}**

**ADOPTED: February 18, 2025**

**VOTE: 7-2**

*City of Lakemont, Illinois*

*\[SEAL\]*

**A RESOLUTION GRANTING CONDITIONAL APPROVAL FOR THE LAKEMONT STATION COMMONS MIXED-USE TRANSIT-ORIENTED DEVELOPMENT PROJECT AT 1400--1550 LAKEMONT STATION BOULEVARD AND AUTHORIZING THE CITY MANAGER TO NEGOTIATE AND EXECUTE A DEVELOPMENT AGREEMENT WITH GRANITE CITY DEVELOPMENT LLC, SUBJECT TO CONDITIONS**

**WHEREAS**, the City of Lakemont, Illinois, is a home rule municipality with a population of approximately 87,000, organized and existing under the Constitution and laws of the State of Illinois, including the broad powers of local self-governance granted under Article VII, Section 6 of the Illinois Constitution of 1970, and is authorized to exercise any power and perform any function pertaining to its government and affairs; and

**WHEREAS**, the City is the owner of certain real property commonly known as 1400--1550 Lakemont Station Boulevard, Lakemont, IL 60048, consisting of three (3) separate parcels of record identified by the following Parcel Identification Numbers: 12-24-300-015, 12-24-300-016, and 12-24-300-017, totaling approximately 12.7 acres (553,212 square feet) (collectively, the \"Property\"). The City acquired Parcels 12-24-300-015 and 12-24-300-016 through tax forfeiture proceedings concluded in 2016, and acquired Parcel 12-24-300-017 by direct purchase in 2018. The Property constitutes the former site of the Lakemont Ironworks manufacturing campus, which ceased industrial operations in 2009 and has remained substantially vacant and underutilized since that time. The Property is situated immediately adjacent to the Lakemont Central Metra commuter rail station, providing direct access to commuter rail service on the Metra Milwaukee District/North Line; and

**WHEREAS**, the Property is currently zoned M-2 (General Manufacturing) under the City of Lakemont Zoning Ordinance, as amended. The proposed mixed-use transit-oriented development contemplated by this Resolution requires the rezoning of the Property from M-2 (General Manufacturing) to PD-TOD (Planned Development--Transit Oriented District) in order to permit the range of residential, commercial, office, hotel, and public uses contemplated by the proposed development program; and

**WHEREAS**, the City, through its Community Development Department under the direction of Paul R. Vasquez, Director, conducted a competitive Request for Proposals (\"RFP\") process for the redevelopment of the Property as a mixed-use transit-oriented development, consistent with the City\'s objectives of leveraging the public investment in the Lakemont Central Metra station, stimulating economic development in the surrounding area, generating tax revenues, creating employment opportunities, and providing housing options at a range of affordability levels. In response to said RFP process, Granite City Development LLC, an Illinois limited liability company formed on March 14, 2019, with its principal office located at 900 West Monroe Street, Suite 2200, Chicago, IL 60607, managed by Marcus J. Pellegrini (\"Developer\"), was selected as the preferred developer based upon its qualifications, experience, project proposal, and demonstrated financial capacity; and

**WHEREAS**, Granite City Development LLC has demonstrated substantial experience in transit-oriented, mixed-use, and affordable housing development, having successfully completed projects including the Halsted Collection (a mixed-use development in Chicago, Illinois), Riverside Crossing (a transit-oriented development in Naperville, Illinois), and Archer Park Residences (an affordable housing development in Chicago, Illinois). The Developer has a reported entity-level net worth of approximately One Hundred Eighty-Five Million Dollars (\$185,000,000), with audited financial statements available through Clearmont Accounting Group LLP, and maintains bonding capacity of up to Seventy-Five Million Dollars (\$75,000,000) through Ridgepoint Surety Corporation; and

**WHEREAS**, the ownership structure of Granite City Development LLC is as follows: sixty percent (60%) of the membership interests are held by the Pellegrini Family Trust; twenty-five percent (25%) of the membership interests are held by Lakeshore Capital Partners Fund III LP, a Delaware limited partnership whose general partner is Lakeshore Capital Management LLC (Managing Partner: Richard \"Rick\" Dominguez), with offices at 200 East Randolph Street, Suite 3100, Chicago, IL 60601; and fifteen percent (15%) of the membership interests are held by TransitWorks Equity Group LLC; and

**WHEREAS**, the Developer has proposed a four-phase mixed-use transit-oriented development to be known as \"Lakemont Station Commons\" (the \"Project\"), with a total gross building area of approximately 1,620,000 square feet, comprising the following program elements and estimated costs:

> *Phase I* --- Three hundred twenty (320) residential dwelling units, consisting of two hundred fifty-six (256) market-rate units and sixty-four (64) affordable housing units available to households earning at or below sixty percent (60%) of the Area Median Income (\"AMI\"); forty-five thousand (45,000) square feet of ground-floor retail space; and a four hundred eighty (480)-space structured parking facility. Phase I shall comprise approximately four hundred twenty thousand (420,000) square feet of gross building area, with an estimated hard construction cost of One Hundred Five Million Dollars (\$105,000,000).
>
> *Phase II* --- Two hundred eighty thousand (280,000) square feet of Class A office space; eighteen thousand (18,000) square feet of retail space; and a three hundred fifty (350)-space structured parking facility. Phase II shall comprise approximately three hundred eighty thousand (380,000) square feet of gross building area, with an estimated hard construction cost of Seventy-Six Million Dollars (\$76,000,000).
>
> *Phase III* --- Two hundred ten (210) residential dwelling units, consisting of one hundred sixty-eight (168) market-rate units and forty-two (42) affordable housing units available to households earning at or below sixty percent (60%) of AMI; and a one hundred fifty (150)-room select-service hotel. Phase III shall comprise approximately four hundred forty thousand (440,000) square feet of gross building area, with an estimated hard construction cost of Eighty-Eight Million Dollars (\$88,000,000).
>
> *Phase IV* --- A 2.5-acre public plaza incorporating landscaping and public art installations; a twenty-eight thousand (28,000) square foot community center facility (to be conveyed to the City upon completion); and a six hundred (600)-space public parking structure. Phase IV shall comprise approximately three hundred eighty thousand (380,000) square feet of gross building area, with an estimated hard construction cost of Seventy-One Million Dollars (\$71,000,000).

The Project, in the aggregate, shall include a total of five hundred thirty (530) residential dwelling units, of which one hundred six (106) shall be affordable housing units (representing approximately twenty percent (20%) of total residential units, thereby meeting the City\'s minimum twenty percent (20%) affordability target); sixty-three thousand (63,000) square feet of total retail space; two hundred eighty thousand (280,000) square feet of Class A office space; one hundred fifty (150) hotel rooms; one thousand four hundred thirty (1,430) structured parking spaces; a 2.5-acre public plaza; and a twenty-eight thousand (28,000) square foot community center. The total estimated hard construction cost for all four phases is approximately Three Hundred Forty Million Dollars (\$340,000,000), and the total estimated Project cost, inclusive of soft costs, is approximately Four Hundred Twelve Million Dollars (\$412,000,000); and

**WHEREAS**, the Developer has proposed the following phasing schedule for the construction of the Project:

> *Phase I:* Groundbreaking no later than March 1, 2026; Substantial Completion by September 30, 2028 (approximately thirty (30) months of construction from groundbreaking).
>
> *Phase II:* Commencement within twelve (12) months of Phase I Substantial Completion; Substantial Completion within twenty-four (24) months of Phase II commencement.
>
> *Phase III:* Commencement within twelve (12) months of Phase II Substantial Completion; Substantial Completion within thirty (30) months of Phase III commencement.
>
> *Phase IV:* Commencement concurrently with Phase III; Substantial Completion within twenty-four (24) months of Phase IV commencement.

; and

**WHEREAS**, the Property was appraised at a fair market value of Eighteen Million Four Hundred Thousand Dollars (\$18,400,000) on an as-is basis by Crossland Appraisal Services in an appraisal report dated January 15, 2025. Pursuant to the City\'s economic development incentive policies and the authority conferred upon the City by its home rule powers, the City proposes to convey the Property to the Developer at a purchase price of Nine Million Two Hundred Thousand Dollars (\$9,200,000), representing fifty percent (50%) of the appraised fair market value, resulting in a land write-down incentive with a value of Nine Million Two Hundred Thousand Dollars (\$9,200,000); and

**WHEREAS**, the Property is located within the Lakemont Central Tax Increment Financing (\"TIF\") District, which was established pursuant to Ordinance No. 2019-42 adopted by the City Council and which expires on December 31, 2042. The City proposes to pledge up to Forty-Eight Million Dollars (\$48,000,000) in tax increment financing revenues over the remaining life of the TIF District to reimburse the Developer for eligible redevelopment project costs, including but not limited to public infrastructure improvements, environmental remediation, and the construction of parking structures, on a pay-as-you-go basis. The estimated annual tax increment generated by the Project at full buildout is approximately Four Million Two Hundred Thousand Dollars (\$4,200,000) per year; and

**WHEREAS**, in addition to the land write-down and tax increment financing incentives described above, the City proposes to provide the following additional public incentives in connection with the Project:

> \(a\) *Infrastructure Cost-Sharing:* The City shall commit Twelve Million Five Hundred Thousand Dollars (\$12,500,000) toward off-site infrastructure improvements, including the widening of Lakemont Station Boulevard, the installation of a new traffic signal at the Oak Street intersection, the extension of water main service, and the upgrade of sanitary sewer capacity. The Developer shall be responsible for Eight Million Three Hundred Thousand Dollars (\$8,300,000) of on-site infrastructure improvements. The total combined infrastructure budget for on-site and off-site improvements is Twenty Million Eight Hundred Thousand Dollars (\$20,800,000).
>
> \(b\) *Property Tax Abatement:* A property tax abatement for Phase I improvements on a twelve (12)-year declining schedule, as follows: Years 1 through 4 --- seventy-five percent (75%) abatement; Years 5 through 8 --- fifty percent (50%) abatement; Years 9 through 12 --- twenty-five percent (25%) abatement; Year 13 and thereafter --- full assessment with no abatement.
>
> \(c\) *Building Permit Fee Waiver:* A waiver of building permit fees for Phase I and Phase II of the Project, with an estimated combined value of One Million Four Hundred Thousand Dollars (\$1,400,000), consisting of Nine Hundred Twenty Thousand Dollars (\$920,000) for Phase I and Four Hundred Eighty Thousand Dollars (\$480,000) for Phase II.

result:

**WHEREAS**, a Phase II Environmental Site Assessment was completed by Greenfield Environmental Consultants LLC in a report dated November 8, 2024. Said assessment identified the following environmental conditions on the Property:

> \(a\) *Parcel 12-24-300-015* --- No environmental contamination requiring further action was identified; this parcel requires no further investigation or remediation.
>
> \(b\) *Parcel 12-24-300-016* --- Petroleum hydrocarbon contamination, specifically benzene, toluene, ethylbenzene, and xylene (\"BTEX\") compounds, was identified in soil and groundwater over an area of approximately 1.8 acres. Remediation under the Illinois Environmental Protection Agency (\"IEPA\") Voluntary Site Remediation Program (\"VSRP\") is required, at an estimated cost of Two Million Eight Hundred Thousand Dollars (\$2,800,000) and an estimated timeline of fourteen (14) to eighteen (18) months, culminating in the issuance of a No Further Remediation (\"NFR\") letter by the IEPA.
>
> \(c\) *Parcel 12-24-300-017* --- Low-level metals contamination in shallow soil consistent with urban fill material was identified. This contamination is suitable for residential use with the installation of an engineered barrier (clean fill cap), at an estimated cost of Three Hundred Forty Thousand Dollars (\$340,000).

The total estimated environmental remediation costs for the Property are Three Million One Hundred Forty Thousand Dollars (\$3,140,000); and

**WHEREAS**, the City Manager, Gerald T. Fong, acting on behalf of the City, and the Developer, by its Manager, Marcus J. Pellegrini, executed a non-binding Negotiated Term Sheet dated January 22, 2025 (the \"Term Sheet\"), setting forth the principal economic and legal terms proposed to govern the development agreement for the Project; and

**WHEREAS**, the Developer has obtained a preliminary (non-binding) commitment letter from First Prairie National Bank for a construction loan facility in an amount of up to Two Hundred Ten Million Dollars (\$210,000,000), subject to the execution of a development agreement in form and substance acceptable to said lender. In addition, the Developer\'s equity sources for the Project include the Pellegrini Family Trust, which has committed Thirty-Four Million Dollars (\$34,000,000), and Lakeshore Capital Partners Fund III LP, which has committed Fifty-One Million Dollars (\$51,000,000), for total committed equity of Eighty-Five Million Dollars (\$85,000,000); and

**WHEREAS**, Corporation Counsel Rebecca A. Sinclair, Esq., and the City\'s outside municipal counsel, Ashford, Keene & Mulvaney LLP, by partner Jonathan Ashford, have reviewed the proposed terms of the development and the Negotiated Term Sheet and have recommended conditional approval of the Project by the City Council, subject to the conditions set forth herein and subject to the preparation and execution of a Development Agreement satisfactory to Corporation Counsel; and

**WHEREAS**, the City Council of the City of Lakemont hereby finds and determines that the proposed Lakemont Station Commons development will advance the public interest of the City and the health, safety, and welfare of its residents by: creating transit-oriented housing and commercial space in close proximity to public transportation; generating substantial property tax revenues for the City, its overlapping taxing districts, and the Lakemont Central TIF District; creating significant construction employment and permanent employment opportunities; providing affordable housing for households at or below sixty percent (60%) of Area Median Income; remediating environmental contamination on formerly blighted and underutilized industrial land; constructing public infrastructure improvements, a public plaza, and a community center to serve the residents of the City; and furthering the goals and objectives of the City\'s Comprehensive Plan, transit-oriented development policies, and economic development strategies.

**NOW, THEREFORE, BE IT RESOLVED** by the City Council of the City of Lakemont, Illinois, as follows:

**Section 1. Incorporation of Recitals.** The above recitals are hereby incorporated into and made a part of this Resolution as though fully set forth herein and are adopted as the findings of the City Council of the City of Lakemont, Illinois.

**Section 2. Conditional Approval Granted; Authorization of City Manager.** The City Council of the City of Lakemont, Illinois, hereby grants conditional approval for the Lakemont Station Commons mixed-use transit-oriented development project as generally described in the recitals hereof and authorizes the City Manager, Gerald T. Fong, to negotiate and execute a Development Agreement (the \"Development Agreement\") with Granite City Development LLC for the development of the Property, subject to the conditions set forth in Section 3 of this Resolution and subject to final review and approval as to form and legality by Corporation Counsel Rebecca A. Sinclair and outside municipal counsel Ashford, Keene & Mulvaney LLP.

**Section 3. Conditions of Approval.** The conditional approval granted by Section 2 of this Resolution and the authority of the City Manager to execute the Development Agreement are expressly subject to the satisfaction of each of the following conditions:

> **(a) Equity Demonstration.** The Developer shall demonstrate, to the reasonable satisfaction of the City Manager and Corporation Counsel, committed equity of not less than Eighty-Five Million Dollars (\$85,000,000) within sixty (60) days of the adoption of this Resolution (i.e., on or before April 19, 2025). Such demonstration shall include executed commitment letters from equity partners, evidence of available and uncommitted capital, bank statements, or other documentation satisfactory to the City in its reasonable discretion, verified by an independent certification from the Developer\'s auditing firm, Clearmont Accounting Group LLP.
>
> **(b) Environmental Remediation Approval.** Prior to any conveyance of the Property or any portion thereof to the Developer, the Developer shall have submitted, and the Illinois Environmental Protection Agency (\"IEPA\") shall have approved, a Remedial Action Plan (\"RAP\") for the environmental remediation of Parcel 12-24-300-016 under the IEPA Voluntary Site Remediation Program (\"VSRP\"). The RAP must comprehensively address the petroleum hydrocarbon contamination, including benzene, toluene, ethylbenzene, and xylene (\"BTEX\") compounds in soil and groundwater, as identified in the Phase II Environmental Site Assessment dated November 8, 2024, prepared by Greenfield Environmental Consultants LLC, and must provide for the issuance of a No Further Remediation (\"NFR\") letter upon completion of all required remediation activities.
>
> **(c) Community Benefits Agreement.** The Development Agreement shall incorporate a Community Benefits Agreement (\"CBA\") or Community Benefits provisions requiring, at a minimum, the following:
>
> \(i\) A local hiring goal requiring that not less than thirty-five percent (35%) of all construction labor hours on the Project be performed by individuals who are residents of the City of Lakemont, Illinois;
>
> \(ii\) A living wage requirement mandating that all permanent employees employed at retail establishments and hotel operations within the Project be compensated at a wage rate of not less than Seventeen Dollars and Fifty Cents (\$17.50) per hour; and
>
> \(iii\) A first-source hiring program under which the Developer and its commercial tenants shall partner with the Lakemont Workforce Center for the recruitment, training, and hiring of individuals to fill permanent employment positions created within the Project.
>
> **(d) Performance Security.** Prior to the conveyance of the Property to the Developer, the Developer shall post a performance bond or irrevocable standby letter of credit in the amount of Fifteen Million Dollars (\$15,000,000), issued by a surety company or financial institution acceptable to the City, securing the Developer\'s obligation to complete Phase I of the Project in accordance with the terms and conditions of the Development Agreement.
>
> **(e) Rezoning and Parcel Consolidation.** The rezoning of the Property from M-2 (General Manufacturing) to PD-TOD (Planned Development--Transit Oriented District) shall be completed, adopted by ordinance, and effective prior to the execution of the Development Agreement. The City Manager is hereby authorized to initiate rezoning proceedings concurrently with the negotiation of the Development Agreement. The three (3) parcels comprising the Property (Parcel IDs 12-24-300-015, 12-24-300-016, and 12-24-300-017) shall be consolidated into a single parcel of record prior to or concurrent with the completion of the rezoning proceedings.
>
> **(f) LEED Gold Certification.** All buildings constructed as part of the Project shall achieve Leadership in Energy and Environmental Design (\"LEED\") Gold certification, or an equivalent green building certification as determined acceptable by the Director of the Community Development Department, Paul R. Vasquez.
>
> **(g) Affordable Housing Minimum.** Not less than twenty percent (20%) of the total residential dwelling units constructed in the Project shall be designated as affordable housing units available to households earning at or below sixty percent (60%) of the Area Median Income (\"AMI\") as determined by the U.S. Department of Housing and Urban Development for the Chicago-Naperville-Elgin Metropolitan Statistical Area, or any successor designation thereof.
>
> **(h) Reversionary Interest / Clawback.** The Developer shall grant to the City a reversionary interest in the Property, such that in the event Phase I of the Project is not Substantially Complete (as such term shall be defined in the Development Agreement) within thirty-six (36) months of the date of conveyance of the Property to the Developer, fee simple title to the Property shall automatically revert to the City, together with all improvements constructed thereon, subject to the City\'s obligation to refund to the Developer the purchase price paid by the Developer for the Property (net of any damages incurred by the City as a result of the Developer\'s failure to timely complete Phase I). The form, terms, and conditions of such reversionary interest shall be set forth in detail in the Development Agreement and shall be recorded against the Property at the time of conveyance.

**Section 4. Authorization of City Manager.** The City Manager, Gerald T. Fong, is hereby authorized and directed to negotiate the terms of the Development Agreement with Granite City Development LLC consistent with the terms described in the recitals hereof and subject to the conditions set forth in Section 3 of this Resolution. The City Manager is further authorized to execute the Development Agreement on behalf of the City of Lakemont, Illinois, upon the satisfaction of all conditions set forth herein and upon approval of the Development Agreement as to form and legality by Corporation Counsel Rebecca A. Sinclair and outside municipal counsel Ashford, Keene & Mulvaney LLP. The City Manager is additionally authorized to execute such ancillary documents, certificates, and instruments as may be necessary or appropriate to effectuate the purposes of this Resolution and the Development Agreement.

**Section 5. Authorization of Rezoning Proceedings.** The City Manager and the Director of the Community Development Department, Paul R. Vasquez, are hereby authorized and directed to initiate proceedings for the rezoning of the Property from M-2 (General Manufacturing) to PD-TOD (Planned Development--Transit Oriented District) and for the consolidation of Parcels 12-24-300-015, 12-24-300-016, and 12-24-300-017 into a single parcel of record, in accordance with applicable provisions of the City of Lakemont Zoning Ordinance and Illinois law.

**Section 6. Directions to Corporation Counsel.** Corporation Counsel Rebecca A. Sinclair and outside municipal counsel Ashford, Keene & Mulvaney LLP are hereby directed to prepare or review, as applicable, the Development Agreement, all ancillary documents and instruments (including but not limited to the deed of conveyance, environmental covenants and access agreements, performance security instruments, community benefits provisions, and any restrictive covenants or declarations), and any rezoning ordinances or amendments to the Zoning Ordinance necessary to implement the purposes of this Resolution.

**Section 7. TIF Compliance.** Any use of tax increment financing revenues in connection with the Project shall comply in all respects with the Illinois Tax Increment Allocation Redevelopment Act (65 ILCS 5/11-74.4-1 *et seq.*) and the Lakemont Central TIF District Redevelopment Plan and Project as adopted by Ordinance No. 2019-42, as the same may be amended from time to time. All TIF-eligible redevelopment project costs shall be determined and reimbursed in accordance with applicable provisions of Illinois law and the terms and conditions of the Development Agreement.

**Section 8. Prevailing Wage.** All construction work performed pursuant to the Development Agreement that constitutes \"public works\" within the meaning of the Illinois Prevailing Wage Act (820 ILCS 130/0.01 *et seq.*) shall be subject to the prevailing wage requirements of said Act. The Development Agreement shall include appropriate prevailing wage compliance provisions, including but not limited to record-keeping, reporting, and certification requirements.

**Section 9. Severability.** If any provision of this Resolution, or the application thereof to any person or circumstance, is held invalid or unenforceable by a court of competent jurisdiction, such invalidity or unenforceability shall not affect other provisions or applications of this Resolution that can be given effect without the invalid or unenforceable provision or application, and to this end the provisions of this Resolution are hereby declared to be severable.

**Section 10. Effective Date.** This Resolution shall be in full force and effect from and after its adoption by the City Council of the City of Lakemont, Illinois.

**ADOPTED** by the City Council of the City of Lakemont, Illinois, this 18th day of February, 2025.

Approximately $58,400,000 in additional debt or mezzanine financing will be required for Phases II through IV. The structure and terms of this additional financing have not yet been determined. Any additional indebtedness secured by the Property or Project assets, and any equity raise that results in dilution of the Pellegrini Interest, shall be subject to the Construction Lender’s prior written consent and the consent rights of Lakeshore Capital Partners Fund III LP under the Lakeshore Side Letter.

This Article 16 implements the Construction Lender's required Development Agreement provisions set forth in Loan Term Sheet Section 12: (a) no reversionary interest (implemented in Article 14); (b) Lender notice and cure rights (Section 16.1); (c) Lender consent to material amendments (Section 16.2); (d) subordination of City interests (Section 16.3); (e) foreclosure/deed-in-lieu assignment (Sections 15.1.4, 16.4); and (f) estoppel certificates (Section 16.5). These provisions are necessary for the Construction Loan to close. The City may resist certain provisions — particularly Section 16.3 (subordination of City interests to the Construction Lender's mortgage) and Section 16.4 (assignment to a foreclosure purchaser without City consent) — as these limit the City's control over the Project following a Developer default. These are open negotiation items with the City. The Lakeshore Side Letter (Section 4.2) also requires Lakeshore's consent for Material Amendments, so amendments to this Agreement will require both Construction Lender and Lakeshore consent (Developer's external and internal constraints). Section 16.2 cross-references the Lakeshore Side Letter's definition of "Material Amendment" to promote consistency.]

The Loan Term Sheet (Section 9(5)) provides that a material default by Developer under this Agreement that is not cured within applicable cure periods, or any termination of this Agreement by the City for any reason, constitutes an Event of Default under the Construction Loan. The cure periods in Article 16.1 are intended to give the Construction Lender time to cure a Developer default before the loan defaults, but Developer should recognize that a City termination of this Agreement would cross-default the loan. Coordinate the cure periods and termination triggers accordingly.]

## ARTICLE 17. INDEMNIFICATION

### Section 17.1 Mutual Indemnification.

Each party shall indemnify, defend, and hold harmless the other party, and its officers, employees, agents, and representatives, from and against all claims, demands, actions, damages, losses, costs, liabilities, and expenses (including reasonable attorneys' fees and court costs) arising from or related to the indemnifying party's negligent acts or omissions, reckless conduct, or willful misconduct in connection with the performance of its obligations under this Agreement.

### Section 17.2 Developer Indemnification of City.

Developer shall indemnify, defend, and hold harmless the City from and against all claims, demands, actions, damages, losses, costs, liabilities, and expenses (including reasonable attorneys' fees) arising from or related to construction activities on the Property, including without limitation claims for personal injury, death, property damage, and workers' compensation, regardless of whether such claims are brought by Developer's employees, contractors, subcontractors, or third parties.

### Section 17.3 City Indemnification of Developer (Environmental).

(a) All construction work performed pursuant to this Agreement that constitutes "public works" within the meaning of the Illinois Prevailing Wage Act, 820 ILCS 130/0.01 et seq. (the "Prevailing Wage Act"), shall be subject to the prevailing wage requirements of the Prevailing Wage Act. This includes, without limitation, the off-site public infrastructure improvements, the on-site infrastructure improvements to the extent TIF-reimbursed, the environmental remediation to the extent TIF-reimbursed, and the Phase IV public parking structure, public plaza, and community center.

(b) Developer shall include prevailing wage requirements in all contracts and subcontracts for construction work subject to the Prevailing Wage Act, shall maintain certified payroll records and make them available to the City and the Illinois Department of Labor upon request, and shall post prevailing wage rate schedules at the construction site.

(c) The parties acknowledge that the scope of Prevailing Wage Act applicability to purely private components of the Project (including market-rate residential, private office, retail, and hotel construction) is a matter of statutory interpretation that may be debatable. The Term Sheet (Section 11.1) states that "all construction performed on the Project shall comply with the Illinois Prevailing Wage Act." To the extent the parties agree to extend prevailing wage coverage beyond the statutorily-required scope as a contractual matter, such extension shall be expressly set forth in Exhibit [__] and Developer shall be entitled to seek corresponding adjustments to the public incentives to offset any incremental cost.

[Drafting Note — Conflict C13 (prevailing wage scope). The Term Sheet (Section 11.1) states that "all construction performed on the Project shall comply with the Illinois Prevailing Wage Act." The TIF Memo (Section V) concludes that, given the substantial public financial assistance (land write-down, TIF, abatement, fee waivers), the Prevailing Wage Act "likely applies to all construction activity on the project site, not merely the TIF-reimbursed components," and recommends the development agreement specify the scope with precision. The TIF Memo notes that the Term Sheet's "all construction" language "converts a potentially debatable statutory question into an unambiguous contractual obligation" and that the Developer "should understand that this provision may increase construction costs for components... that might otherwise be argued to fall outside the statutory mandate." Developer's position, reflected in Section 9.3, is to (i) comply with the Prevailing Wage Act where it applies by statute, (ii) flag the debatability of full-project coverage, and (iii) seek offsetting incentive adjustments if the parties contractually extend coverage to purely private components. The City's strongest position (per the TIF Memo) is broad application. This is a significant cost open item; the Lakeshore Side Letter (Section 3.5) prohibits Developer from agreeing to terms materially increasing total Project costs beyond $412,000,000 without Investor consent, so any contractual extension of prevailing wage that materially increases cost may require Lakeshore consent.]

### Section 9.4 Insurance.

Developer shall maintain, or cause its general contractor to maintain, throughout the construction period for each Phase, the following minimum insurance coverages: (a) Commercial General Liability (CGL) — $10,000,000 per occurrence and $25,000,000 in the aggregate; (b) Umbrella/Excess Liability — $25,000,000; (c) Builder's Risk — full replacement value of each Phase under construction; (d) Workers' Compensation — statutory limits; (e) Automobile Liability — $5,000,000 combined single limit; and (f) Professional Liability (Architect/Engineer) — $5,000,000. The City and the Construction Lender shall be named as additional insureds/loss payees, as applicable. All carriers shall be rated A- VII or better by A.M. Best. All policies shall provide not less than 30 days' prior written notice of cancellation or material modification.

[Drafting Note — Conflict C9 (CGL aggregate). The Term Sheet (Section 11.3) requires CGL coverage of $10,000,000 per occurrence and $20,000,000 in the aggregate. The Loan Term Sheet (Section 5) requires CGL coverage of $10,000,000 per occurrence and $25,000,000 in the aggregate. Section 9.4 adopts the higher aggregate ($25,000,000) required by the Construction Lender, which also satisfies the Term Sheet. The Term Sheet's lower aggregate is superseded by the Construction Lender's requirement. Confirm the City accepts the $25,000,000 aggregate.]

### Section 9.5 Construction Monitoring.

Developer shall submit monthly draw requests supported by AIA G702/G703 documentation to the Construction Lender and shall provide quarterly construction progress reports to the City's Community Development Department, including percentage of completion, updated schedule, and identification of any delays, within thirty (30) days following the end of each calendar quarter during which active construction is occurring.

## ARTICLE 10. COMMUNITY BENEFITS

### Section 10.1 Local Hiring.

Developer shall use commercially reasonable efforts to achieve a goal of 35% Lakemont residents for construction workforce employment across all Phases of the Project, measured on a cumulative project-wide basis. Developer shall submit quarterly reports to the City's Community Development Department documenting local hiring percentages within thirty (30) days following the end of each calendar quarter during which active construction is occurring. Failure to achieve the 35% local hiring goal in any given quarter or on a cumulative basis shall not, in and of itself, constitute a default under this Agreement, provided Developer demonstrates good-faith efforts to achieve the goal, including posting job openings with the City's workforce development partners, attending local job fairs, and providing reasonable notice of hiring opportunities to the Community Development Department.

[Drafting Note — Conflict C19 (local hiring — goal vs. requirement). The Term Sheet (Section 9) frames the 35% local hiring commitment as a goal measured on a cumulative project-wide basis, with good-faith compliance and an express statement that failure to achieve the goal "shall not, in and of itself, constitute a default." Resolution Condition #8(c)(i), however, refers to "a local hiring goal requiring that not less than thirty-five percent (35%)." Developer's position, reflected in Section 10.1, is that the 35% commitment is a good-faith goal, not a hard requirement, and that missing the goal is not a default if good-faith efforts are demonstrated. The City may seek to tighten this; reconcile the language.]
